Lateral Condyle of Tibia (palpate the broad bony prominence on the lateral proximal tibia — the tibialis anterior originates from the lateral tibial condyle and upper tibial shaft)
placement: Position the patient supine with the leg extended and the ankle in neutral.
Identify the proximal third of the anterior leg.
Palpate the sharp anterior crest of the tibia — the prominent subcutaneous ridge on the shin.
Move your finger 2 cm lateral to the tibial crest — the tibialis anterior is the largest muscle bulk here.
Ask the patient to dorsiflex the foot against resistance to confirm the muscle belly under your fingers.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the largest muscle bulk, approximately 2 cm lateral to the tibial crest.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle belly.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: The tibialis anterior is superficial and easily identified — this is a straightforward injection.
Avoid the anterior tibial artery, which runs deep to the muscle near the interosseous membrane.
The deep peroneal nerve runs alongside the anterior tibial artery — it is generally protected by the muscle itself.

⚠️ STOP — DO NOT INJECT in typical UMN spasticity. The tibialis anterior is the primary ankle dorsiflexor and is almost always WEAK in upper-motor-neuron syndromes (it's the muscle that fails in foot drop). Injecting it will cause or worsen drop foot.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the proximal anterolateral leg, 2 cm lateral to the tibial crest
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue — it is thin on the anterior leg.
The Tibialis Anterior appears as a large hypoechoic muscle with a prominent central tendon in the distal portion, located immediately lateral to the tibial cortex.
The hyperechoic tibial cortex is visible medially as a bright flat line.
The Extensor Digitorum Longus is visible laterally, separated by a fascial septum.
Deep to the tibialis anterior, the interosseous membrane appears as a bright hyperechoic line connecting the tibia and fibula — the anterior tibial vessels run just anterior to this.
safetyNotes: The anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve run deep to the muscle on the interosseous membrane — do not advance the needle to this depth.
The superficial peroneal nerve runs in the lateral compartment — avoid directing the needle too far laterally.
The tibial cortex is immediately medial — contacting the periosteum is painful; angle the needle slightly laterally.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, leg extended. Place probe transversely on proximal anterolateral leg, 2 cm lateral to tibial crest. Photo from front showing probe on anterior shin.
pearls: ⚠️ DO NOT INJECT in typical UMN spasticity — this muscle is almost always weak, not spastic. Injecting it causes foot drop.
Rare exception: focal dystonia with confirmed EMG over-activity in TA, with neurology / physiatry consultation. Even then, very low doses only.
Listed here for anatomic reference and to warn against injection. PROTECT this muscle's function for foot clearance during swing phase.
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Tibialis Posterior
landmarks: Medial Tibial Border (palpate the sharp subcutaneous tibial edge — the tibialis posterior lies on the posterior surface of the interosseous membrane, deep to the superficial posterior compartment muscles)
Fibular Head (palpate the bony knob on the proximal lateral leg — helps orient the interosseous membrane between tibia and fibula)
Intermalleolar Line (draw an imaginary line between the medial and lateral malleoli — the midpoint of the leg between the fibular head and this line approximates the mid-belly)
placement: Position the patient prone or supine with the leg externally rotated.
Identify the midpoint between the fibular head and the intermalleolar line on the medial leg.
Approach from the medial side of the leg, posterior to the medial tibial border.
Insert the needle directed posterolaterally, sliding along the posterior aspect of the tibia.
Advance through the superficial posterior compartment muscles (Gastrocnemius/Soleus) toward the interosseous membrane.
The tibialis posterior lies directly on the interosseous membrane — it is the deepest muscle in this compartment.
Typical depth is 3-5 cm depending on body habitus — USG confirms placement.
Aspirate before injecting. Confirm the needle is in the tibialis posterior and not in overlying muscles.
setup: USG is MANDATORY — high risk of posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve injury.
The tibialis posterior is the deepest muscle in the deep posterior compartment.
Use an in-plane needle approach for continuous visualization during this deep injection.
Have the patient invert the foot against resistance to confirm the muscle on ultrasound — this is the strongest foot invertor.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz) in thin patients; lower-frequency linear (6-9 MHz) or curvilinear (5-8 MHz) is often required in average/larger patients due to muscle depth (3-5+ cm)
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the medial mid-leg, posterior to the tibial border
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and the crural fascia superficially.
The Gastrocnemius and Soleus form the superficial posterior compartment — they appear as hypoechoic muscle layers with a fascial septum between them.
Deep to the Soleus, the posterior tibial vessels (pulsatile anechoic circles) and tibial nerve (hyperechoic honeycomb) are visible in the deep posterior compartment.
The Tibialis Posterior appears as a hypoechoic muscle belly lying directly on the hyperechoic interosseous membrane, deep to the neurovascular bundle.
The bright cortical lines of the tibia (medially) and fibula (laterally) frame the compartment — the interosseous membrane connects them.
safetyNotes: The posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve run in the deep posterior compartment directly superficial to the tibialis posterior — always identify them with color Doppler.
The peroneal artery runs between the tibialis posterior and flexor hallucis longus laterally — use color Doppler.
This is a high-risk injection — do not attempt without real-time ultrasound guidance and confident identification of all neurovascular structures.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone or supine with leg externally rotated. Place probe transversely on medial mid-leg, just behind tibia. Photo from medial side showing probe behind tibial border.
pearls: The #1 cause of varus foot in spasticity — always assess this muscle
Deep compartment — longer needle needed. Use US to navigate past soleus
Posterior tibial artery runs alongside — identify it with color Doppler before injecting
supplies: Ultrasound machine with linear probe (high-frequency for thin patients; lower-frequency or curvilinear for deeper anatomy)
22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)

name: Flexor Hallucis Longus (FHL)
landmarks: Fibula — distal shaft (palpate the lateral leg bone in its distal third — the FHL originates from the posterior fibula and lies posteromedial to it)
Achilles Tendon (palpate the thick cord at the posterior ankle — the FHL belly lies deep and lateral in the distal calf, anterior to the Achilles)
placement: Position the patient prone with the feet hanging off the edge of the bed.
Identify the distal third of the posterior leg, lateral aspect.
The FHL belly is located posteromedial to the fibula, deep to the Soleus and lateral Gastrocnemius.
Approach from the lateral side to avoid the neurovascular bundle, which runs medially.
Insert the needle lateral to the Achilles tendon, directed toward the posterior fibula.
Advance 2-4 cm under ultrasound guidance to pass through the Soleus and enter the FHL belly.
Confirm placement by asking the patient to flex the great toe — you should see movement of the muscle on ultrasound.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: USG is MANDATORY — the FHL is a deep muscle and the neurovascular bundle runs medially.
A lateral approach minimizes risk to the posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve.
Have the patient flex the great toe against resistance to identify the FHL on ultrasound.
The FHL is smaller than the FDL — use precise dosing.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the distal posterolateral calf, adjacent to the fibula
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and the Soleus muscle/aponeurosis superficially.
The fibula appears as a bright hyperechoic cortical line laterally with posterior acoustic shadowing.
The FHL appears as a hypoechoic muscle belly lying posteromedial to the fibula, deep to the Soleus.
Ask the patient to flex the great toe — dynamic movement confirms the FHL.
Medially, the posterior tibial vessels and tibial nerve are visible — these must be identified and avoided.
safetyNotes: The posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve lie medial to the FHL — always use a lateral approach.
The peroneal artery runs between the FHL and tibialis posterior — identify with color Doppler.
The sural nerve runs superficially in the posterior calf — avoid the superficial subcutaneous plane.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, feet off edge. Place probe transversely on distal posterolateral calf, adjacent to fibula. Photo from behind showing probe on distal lateral calf near fibula.
pearls: Key target for great toe hyperflexion (claw hallux) pattern — do NOT confuse with hitchhiker's toe (striatal toe), which is caused by EHL spasticity.
Located lateral to FDL in the deep posterior compartment
Small muscle, conservative dosing — over-injection causes toe weakness during push-off
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Extensor Hallucis Longus (EHL)
landmarks: Anterior Tibia (palpate the anterior tibial crest — the EHL lies in the anterior compartment lateral to the tibialis anterior)
Distal Fibula (palpate the lateral malleolus and trace the fibula proximally — the EHL originates from the middle third of the anterior fibula)
placement: Position the patient supine with the leg extended and the ankle in neutral.
Identify the distal third of the anterior leg, approximately 10 cm proximal to the ankle joint.
Ask the patient to extend (dorsiflex) the great toe — the EHL tendon will become visible and palpable on the dorsum of the foot.
Trace the tendon proximally to locate the muscle belly, which lies between the Tibialis Anterior (medially) and Extensor Digitorum Longus (laterally).
Palpate the gap between these two muscle bellies — the EHL is in this interval.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the EHL muscle belly.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle substance.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: Palpate the EHL tendon during active hallux extension to trace it proximally to the muscle belly.
The EHL is a relatively small muscle — precise needle placement is important.
The anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve run in the anterior compartment near the EHL — use caution.
USG can confirm placement and identify the neurovascular structures.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the distal anterolateral leg, approximately 10 cm proximal to the ankle
viewSteps: Identify the thin subcutaneous tissue on the anterior leg.
The Tibialis Anterior is the large hypoechoic muscle medially, adjacent to the tibial cortex.
The EHL appears as a smaller hypoechoic muscle belly between the Tibialis Anterior and the Extensor Digitorum Longus.
The anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve appear as an anechoic pulsatile circle and hyperechoic structure on the interosseous membrane, adjacent to the EHL.
The fibula is visible laterally as a hyperechoic cortical line.
safetyNotes: The anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve run immediately adjacent to the EHL — identify them with color Doppler before injecting.
The EHL is a small muscle; avoid injecting into the adjacent Tibialis Anterior or Extensor Digitorum Longus by confirming identity with dynamic toe extension.
The interosseous membrane lies deep — do not advance the needle to this level.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, leg extended. Place probe transversely on distal anterolateral leg, ~10 cm above ankle. Photo from front showing probe on anterior distal leg.
pearls: Causes great toe hyperextension (hitchhiker's toe/striatal toe)
Thin muscle — easy to over-inject. Start with very low doses (10-25U Botox)
Runs between tibialis anterior and EDL — identify all three on US
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Adductor Hallucis
landmarks: 1st and 2nd Metatarsal Heads — Plantar (palpate the ball of the foot under the great toe and second toe — the oblique head of adductor hallucis lies in the web space between these two metatarsals)
placement: Position the patient supine with the foot accessible, or prone with the foot dorsiflexed.
Palpate the 1st and 2nd metatarsal heads on the plantar surface — these are the bony prominences under the ball of the foot.
For the oblique head: the injection site is in the web space between the 1st and 2nd metatarsals on the plantar surface.
A dorsal approach is an alternative: insert the needle from the dorsum of the foot between the 1st and 2nd metatarsal shafts, directed plantarward.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the foot surface into the small muscle belly.
Advance 1-1.5 cm — this is a small intrinsic foot muscle.
For the transverse head: the muscle runs across the MTP joints — inject at the level of the metatarsal heads.
Aspirate before injecting. Use a small-gauge needle (25G or smaller) given the tight anatomic space.
setup: This is an advanced injection — the anatomy of the intrinsic foot muscles is compact and complex.
USG guidance is recommended to navigate the small intrinsic space and avoid neurovascular structures.
A dorsal approach may be less painful for the patient than a plantar approach.
Consider using EMG guidance as an adjunct to confirm placement in this small muscle.

ultrasound.orientation: Longitudinal along the 1st-2nd intermetatarsal space on the plantar surface, or transverse across the metatarsal heads
viewSteps: Identify the hyperechoic cortex of the 1st and 2nd metatarsal shafts as bright lines with posterior shadowing.
Between the metatarsals, the Adductor Hallucis (oblique head) appears as a small hypoechoic muscle belly in the interosseous space.
The flexor tendons may be visible as hyperechoic linear structures on the plantar side of the metatarsals.
The plantar digital nerves and arteries appear as small hyperechoic and anechoic structures running between the metatarsal heads.
safetyNotes: The plantar digital nerves run between the metatarsal heads — interdigital neuromas are common here; identify nerve tissue before injecting.
The plantar metatarsal arteries run in the interosseous spaces — use color Doppler to identify them.
The first dorsal interosseous muscle lies dorsal to the adductor hallucis — ensure you are in the correct plane.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, foot accessible. Place probe longitudinally along 1st-2nd intermetatarsal space on plantar surface. Photo showing probe on plantar forefoot between 1st and 2nd metatarsals.
pearls: Target for hallux valgus/adduction pattern in spastic foot
Very small muscle in the plantar foot — use hockey-stick probe if available
Low doses only — 10-25U Botox typically sufficient
dosageNote: Very small intrinsic foot muscle. Low doses only.

name: Triceps
landmarks: Acromion (palpate the bony shelf at the tip of the shoulder — follow the spine of the scapula laterally to its most lateral point)
Olecranon (palpate the prominent bony point at the posterior elbow — this is the insertion of the triceps tendon)
Posterior Midline of Humerus (draw an imaginary line from the acromion to the olecranon along the posterior arm — the muscle belly is centered here)
placement: Position the patient seated or prone with the arm resting on a support, elbow flexed to approximately 90 degrees.
Identify the posterior midpoint of the humerus (halfway between the acromion and the olecranon).
Palpate the triceps muscle belly — ask the patient to extend the elbow against resistance to confirm the bulk of the long and lateral heads.
For the long head: insert the needle into the medial portion of the posterior arm at mid-humeral level, perpendicular to the skin.
For the lateral head: insert the needle into the lateral portion of the posterior arm, slightly more proximal, perpendicular to the skin.
Advance 1.5-2 cm into the muscle belly — the triceps is a large, superficial muscle on the posterior arm.
Avoid the medial aspect of the distal arm where the ulnar nerve courses in the ulnar groove behind the medial epicondyle.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute injections across both heads if needed, spacing sites 3-4 cm apart.
setup: Surface anatomy is usually adequate given the large, easily palpable muscle belly.
USG can help distinguish the long head from the lateral head and confirm depth.
Have the patient extend the elbow against resistance to identify and mark the muscle belly before injection.
The radial nerve courses in the spiral groove of the humerus on the posterolateral aspect — stay in the central or medial muscle belly at the mid-humeral level to avoid it.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the posterior arm at the mid-humeral level
viewSteps: Position the patient prone or seated with the arm supported. Place the probe transversely on the posterior arm.
The Long Head of the Triceps appears as the large, prominent hypoechoic muscle — it is the most superficial and medial of the three triceps heads at the mid-arm level.
The humerus appears as a bright curved hyperechoic line with posterior acoustic shadowing deep to the triceps.
CRITICAL: The Radial Nerve appears as a hyperechoic oval structure at the deep border of the triceps, in the spiral groove of the humerus — it sits between the triceps and the bone.
At more proximal levels, all three heads (long, lateral, medial) can be distinguished by their fascial separations.
To confirm the long head: have the patient extend the elbow against resistance — the long head contracts prominently.
safetyNotes: The Radial Nerve runs in the spiral groove of the humerus, directly deep to the triceps — ALWAYS identify it before injecting.
Inject into the superficial-to-mid portion of the muscle belly — do not advance the needle to the bone.
The profunda brachii artery accompanies the radial nerve — use color Doppler to confirm.
The long head is safest to inject in the mid-substance away from the spiral groove.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Long Head of Triceps Spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated or prone. Place probe transversely on posterior mid-upper arm. Photo from behind showing probe across triceps belly.
pearls: Rarely injected in spasticity — extended elbow is uncommon. Confirm the pattern
If injecting, protect elbow extension function as much as possible for reaching
Long head crosses the shoulder — may contribute to shoulder extension posturing
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Rarely injected for spasticity. Confirm genuine spasticity before treating.

name: Brachioradialis
landmarks: Lateral Epicondyle (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral distal humerus — it is the origin landmark for the mobile wad of muscles)
Radial Styloid (palpate the bony prominence at the distal lateral wrist — the brachioradialis inserts at the base of the styloid process)
Lateral Supracondylar Ridge (palpate the bony ridge running proximally from the lateral epicondyle along the lateral humerus — the muscle originates from its distal two-thirds)
placement: Position the patient seated with the forearm resting on a table in neutral position (thumb pointing upward).
Identify the proximal third of the forearm on the lateral (radial) side.
Palpate the brachioradialis muscle belly — ask the patient to flex the elbow against resistance with the forearm in neutral to confirm its contraction.
The muscle belly is the most superficial and lateral muscle of the forearm, forming the lateral border of the cubital fossa.
Insert the needle into the bulk of the muscle belly at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds of the forearm, perpendicular to the skin.
Advance 1-1.5 cm — the muscle is superficial.
Stay lateral and superficial to avoid the radial nerve/posterior interosseous nerve, which courses deep to the brachioradialis.
Aspirate before injecting. A single injection into the mid-belly is usually sufficient.
setup: Surface anatomy is usually sufficient — the muscle belly is easily palpable when the patient flexes the elbow with the forearm in neutral.
USG can confirm the needle is in brachioradialis and not in the deeper supinator or extensor muscles.
Mark the muscle belly with the forearm in neutral and the elbow flexed against resistance.

The radial nerve bifurcates into the superficial (sensory) branch and the posterior interosseous nerve (motor) deep to this muscle at the level of the radial head — stay proximal in the muscle belly and superficial.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz), Samsung LA3-16AD recommended
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the lateral proximal forearm, approximately 4-6 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the lateral forearm with the patient's elbow slightly flexed and forearm in neutral (thumb-up) position.
Identify the Brachioradialis as the most superficial muscle on the lateral side — it appears as a crescent-shaped hypoechoic structure directly under the subcutaneous tissue.
Deep and lateral to the Brachioradialis, identify the ECRL (Extensor Carpi Radialis Longus) — it sits adjacent to and partially beneath the BR.
Deep to both BR and ECRL, the large Brachialis muscle fills the deeper compartment wrapping around the lateral humerus.
CRITICAL LANDMARK: The Radial Nerve appears as a small hyperechoic oval structure in the fascial plane between the Brachioradialis and Brachialis — typically at the lateral-deep border of the BR.
To confirm identification: ask the patient to flex the elbow with the forearm in neutral — the BR will contract and thicken while the ECRL performs wrist extension.
safetyNotes: The Radial Nerve runs in the intermuscular septum between the Brachioradialis and Brachialis — always identify it before injecting.
Keep the needle tip superficial and within the BR muscle belly — do not advance past the fascial plane toward the radial nerve.
The radial recurrent artery may run near the radial nerve — use color Doppler if uncertain.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Brachioradialis spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, forearm neutral on table. Place probe transversely on lateral proximal forearm, 4-6 cm below lateral epicondyle. Photo from lateral view showing probe on lateral forearm.
pearls: Key contributor to elbow flexion often missed — it's a forearm muscle that flexes the elbow
Best identified on US with forearm in neutral (thumb up) position
Co-inject with biceps and brachialis for refractory elbow flexion
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Superficial forearm muscle. Stay lateral and superficial to avoid radial nerve.

name: Pronator Teres
landmarks: Medial Epicondyle (palpate the prominent bony point on the medial distal humerus — the humeral head of pronator teres originates here along with the common flexor tendon)
Cubital Fossa (palpate the triangular depression on the anterior elbow — the pronator teres forms the medial border of this fossa)
Radial Tuberosity (not directly palpable, but located on the medial proximal radius approximately 3-4 cm distal to the elbow crease — the muscle crosses obliquely to insert on the mid-lateral radius)
placement: Position the patient seated with the forearm supinated and the elbow extended, resting on a support surface.
Identify the medial epicondyle and the proximal medial forearm.
Palpate the pronator teres — ask the patient to pronate the forearm against resistance while you feel the muscle belly contract just distal to the medial epicondyle.
The muscle belly runs obliquely from the medial epicondyle toward the mid-lateral radius.
Insert the needle 2-3 cm distal to the medial epicondyle, into the bulk of the muscle belly on the medial-to-anterior proximal forearm.
Advance the needle 1-1.5 cm perpendicular to the skin.
The median nerve passes between the two heads of pronator teres — stay superficial in the muscle bulk and do not advance deep toward the ulna.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: USG is recommended to identify the median nerve, which passes between the humeral and ulnar heads of the pronator teres.
Have the patient pronate and supinate the forearm against resistance to confirm the muscle belly location.
Mark the injection site 2-3 cm distal and slightly lateral to the medial epicondyle.
The median nerve and brachial/ulnar arteries are in close proximity — ultrasound guidance significantly improves safety.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz), Samsung LA3-16AD recommended
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the medial proximal forearm, 3-5 cm distal to the medial epicondyle
viewSteps: Position the patient with the elbow extended and forearm supinated to stretch the pronator teres.
Place the probe transversely on the medial proximal forearm just distal to the elbow crease.
The Pronator Teres appears as a large, round/oval hypoechoic muscle occupying the superficial-to-mid compartment — it is one of the most prominent structures at this level.
Superficial and slightly lateral to the PT, identify the FCR (Flexor Carpi Radialis) — it appears as a smaller oval muscle.
The median nerve and ulnar artery pass deep to the PT — they are not always visible but should be assumed to be in the deep plane.
To confirm: ask the patient to pronate the forearm against resistance — the PT will visibly contract and become more echogenic on US.
safetyNotes: The median nerve passes between the two heads of the pronator teres — inject into the superficial muscle belly only.
The ulnar artery courses deep to the PT — use Doppler if injecting near the deep border.
Avoid the medial epicondyle region where the common flexor tendon origin is located.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Pronator teres spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, forearm supinated. Place probe transversely on medial proximal forearm, 3-5 cm below medial epicondyle. Photo showing probe on medial proximal forearm near elbow.
pearls: Primary target for forearm pronation spasticity
Median nerve passes between its two heads — identify on US and avoid
Often co-injected with FCR and FCU for combined wrist/forearm pattern
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Compact muscle. 1-2 sites in proximal belly.

name: Flexor Carpi Radialis
landmarks: Medial Epicondyle (palpate the bony prominence on the medial distal humerus — the FCR originates from the common flexor tendon here)

FCR Tendon at Wrist (palpate the prominent tendon on the anterior wrist just radial to midline — ask the patient to flex the wrist against resistance; the FCR tendon is the most radial of the two prominent wrist flexor tendons)

Palmaris Longus Tendon (palpate the most prominent midline tendon at the anterior wrist with wrist flexion — the FCR lies immediately radial to it; if palmaris longus is absent, the FCR is the single prominent tendon)
placement: Position the patient seated with the forearm supinated and resting on a flat surface.
Identify the proximal to mid-forearm on the anterior (volar) surface.
Palpate the FCR muscle belly — ask the patient to flex the wrist against resistance while palpating the proximal forearm, 4-5 cm distal to the medial epicondyle and slightly radial to midline.
The muscle belly is located radial to the palmaris longus and superficial to the flexor digitorum superficialis.
Insert the needle into the bulk of the muscle belly at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds of the forearm, perpendicular to the skin.
Advance 1-1.5 cm — the FCR is a relatively superficial muscle.
Stay superficial and radial to avoid the median nerve, which lies deep between the FCR and the flexor digitorum superficialis.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: USG is recommended to distinguish FCR from adjacent flexor muscles and to identify the median nerve.
Have the patient flex the wrist against resistance and palpate the FCR tendon at the wrist, then trace it proximally to the muscle belly.
Mark the injection site before proceeding.
The radial artery runs lateral to the FCR tendon in the distal forearm — stay in the proximal muscle belly to avoid this vessel.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz), Samsung LA3-16AD recommended
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the central volar forearm, at the proximal-to-mid third
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the volar forearm, centered over the palpable FCR tendon.
The FCR appears as a superficial, triangular/oval hypoechoic muscle directly under the antebrachial fascia — it is one of the most superficial forearm flexors.
Deep to the FCR, the FDS appears as a broader muscle layer.
CRITICAL: The Median Nerve appears as a hyperechoic honeycomb oval between the FDS and FDP, typically just deep and slightly ulnar to the FCR.
Further deep, the FDP occupies the deepest volar compartment.
To confirm: ask the patient to flex the wrist with radial deviation — the FCR tendon will become taut and the muscle belly will contract.
safetyNotes: The Median Nerve lies deep to the FCR — it is typically between the FDS and FDP layers just ulnar to the FCR.
The radial artery runs lateral to the FCR tendon distally — use Doppler if in doubt.
The FCR is relatively superficial, so a shallow needle depth (1-1.5 cm) is usually sufficient.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Flexor Carpi Radialis Spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, forearm supinated on table. Place probe transversely on central volar forearm at proximal-to-mid third. Photo from above showing probe on volar mid-forearm.
pearls: Main contributor to wrist flexion and radial deviation
Radial artery runs alongside — identify on US with color Doppler
One of the most commonly injected forearm muscles for wrist flexion pattern
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Flexor Carpi Ulnaris
landmarks: Medial Epicondyle (palpate the prominent bony point on the medial distal humerus — the humeral head of FCU originates from the common flexor tendon here)
Olecranon (palpate the bony prominence at the posterior elbow — the ulnar head of FCU originates from the medial border of the olecranon and proximal ulna)

Pisiform Bone (palpate the small round bony prominence at the base of the hypothenar eminence on the ulnar-volar wrist — the FCU tendon inserts here; it is easily felt with the wrist in slight flexion and ulnar deviation)
placement: Position the patient seated with the forearm supinated or in neutral, resting on a support.
Identify the muscle belly on the ulnar (medial) aspect of the proximal to mid-forearm.
Palpate the FCU — ask the patient to flex and ulnarly deviate the wrist against resistance; the FCU belly contracts on the medial forearm.
The FCU is the most medial (ulnar) of the superficial forearm flexors.
Insert the needle into the bulk of the muscle belly at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds of the forearm, on the medial (ulnar) aspect.
Advance 1-1.5 cm perpendicular to the skin — the muscle is superficial.
The ulnar nerve travels between the two heads of the FCU proximally and then runs deep to the muscle along its ulnar border — stay in the central muscle belly.
Aspirate before injecting.
Inject in the PROXIMAL THIRD of the forearm only — distally, the ulnar nerve becomes increasingly superficial as it approaches Guyon's canal at the wrist. Avoid the distal half.
setup: USG is recommended to identify the ulnar nerve, which runs deep to and along the ulnar border of the FCU.
Have the patient flex the wrist with ulnar deviation against resistance to identify the muscle belly.
Trace the FCU tendon from the pisiform bone proximally to locate the muscle belly.
The ulnar artery runs alongside the ulnar nerve deep to the FCU — ultrasound guidance helps avoid both structures.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the medial forearm, forearm supinated
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the medial (ulnar) side of the forearm with the forearm fully supinated.
The FCU is the most superficial and most medial muscle — it appears as a rounded hypoechoic muscle directly under the skin on the ulnar border.
CRITICAL: The Ulnar Nerve appears as a small hyperechoic oval structure at the deep-medial border of the FCU, between the FCU and the FDP.
Deep to the FCU, identify the FDP as the larger deep muscle layer.
Lateral to the FCU, the Pronator Teres (PT) may be visible in the proximal forearm.
In longitudinal view: the FCU appears as a superficial band (red overlay) with the FDP as the deeper band beneath.
To confirm: ask the patient to flex the wrist with ulnar deviation — the FCU contracts prominently.
safetyNotes: The Ulnar Nerve is immediately deep to the FCU — it is the single most important structure to identify before injecting.
Inject into the superficial portion of the FCU only, keeping the needle away from the deep fascial border where the ulnar nerve runs.
The ulnar artery runs alongside the ulnar nerve — use color Doppler to confirm the neurovascular bundle location.

The ulnar nerve emerges from beneath FCU in the distal third of the forearm and becomes superficial near Guyon's canal. Confine injections to the proximal half/third where the nerve is still protected deep to the muscle.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Flexor Carpi Ulnaris Spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, forearm supinated. Place probe transversely on medial forearm. Photo showing probe on ulnar/medial side of mid-forearm.
pearls: Main contributor to wrist flexion and ulnar deviation
Ulnar nerve and artery run in Guyon's canal nearby — identify on US
Combined FCR + FCU injection gives best results for wrist flexion
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Inject proximal third only — ulnar nerve becomes superficial distally.

name: Flexor Digitorum Superficialis
pattern: Finger flexion at PIP joints, Wrist flexion
landmarks: Medial Epicondyle (palpate the bony prominence on the medial distal humerus — the humeroulnar head of FDS originates from the common flexor tendon here)
Coronoid Process of Ulna (not directly palpable, but located approximately 2 cm distal to the elbow crease on the medial side — the ulnar head of FDS originates here)

Proximal Radius (palpate the radial head by pronating and supinating the forearm while pressing just distal to the lateral epicondyle — the radial head of FDS originates from the proximal radial shaft)
placement: Position the patient seated with the forearm supinated and resting on a flat surface.
Identify the mid-forearm on the anterior (volar) surface.
Palpate the FDS — ask the patient to flex the fingers at the PIP joints while the DIP joints remain extended (isolates FDS from FDP). Feel the contraction in the mid-anterior forearm.
The FDS lies deep to the FCR, palmaris longus, and FCU, but superficial to the FDP.
Insert the needle at the mid-forearm level, between the FCR and FCU, slightly deeper than the superficial flexor layer.
Advance 1.5-2 cm perpendicular to the skin to pass through the superficial flexor layer and into the FDS.
The median nerve runs on the superficial surface of the FDS (between the FDS and the superficial flexors) — approach from the ulnar side to minimize risk.
Aspirate before injecting. Multiple injection sites along the forearm may be needed given the broad muscle belly.
setup: USG is highly recommended — the FDS lies in the intermediate layer and is difficult to distinguish from surrounding muscles by palpation alone.
Have the patient flex the PIP joints with the DIP joints extended to confirm FDS activation.
The FDS has four separate muscle bellies for digits 2-5; target the belly corresponding to the affected digits.
The median nerve travels on the superficial surface of the FDS — always identify it with ultrasound before injecting.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the volar forearm, 5-8 cm distal to the medial epicondyle
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the central volar forearm.
The FDS is the broad, flat muscle layer in the superficial volar compartment — it appears as a wide hypoechoic band directly under the subcutaneous tissue and fascia.
Deep to the FDS, the FDP occupies the deeper compartment — a clear fascial plane separates the two.
CRITICAL: The Median Nerve appears as a honeycomb-pattern hyperechoic oval at the border between FDS and FDP, typically on the radial (lateral) side.
The Ulnar Nerve appears at the medial edge of the field, between the FDS/FCU and the deeper FDP.
To confirm identification: ask the patient to flex the PIP joints of the fingers with the DIP joints held straight — the FDS will contract independently of FDP.
Note the video annotation: 'VIEW OF FDS ON TOP OF FDP WITH MEDIAN NERVE AND ULNAR NERVE DELIMITATION'
safetyNotes: The Median Nerve lies directly at the deep border of the FDS — inject into the superficial-to-mid portion of the muscle belly.
The Ulnar Nerve runs along the medial border between FDS and FCU — avoid the medial edge.
Keep the needle superficial within the FDS — the nerve-vessel plane lies at the FDS-FDP interface.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Flexor digitorum superficialis spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, forearm supinated on table. Place probe transversely on volar forearm, 5-8 cm below medial epicondyle. Photo showing probe on central volar forearm.
pearls: Target for finger flexion at PIP joints
Lies between FCR/FCU superficially and FDP deep — be precise on US
Consider injecting specific slips if only certain fingers are affected
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Dose per head if targeting individual fingers.

name: Flexor Digitorum Profundus
pattern: Finger flexion at DIP joints, Wrist flexion
landmarks: Medial Epicondyle and Proximal Ulna (palpate the medial epicondyle and run your finger distally along the subcutaneous medial border of the ulna — the FDP originates from the proximal three-quarters of the ulna and the interosseous membrane)
Subcutaneous Ulnar Border (palpate the sharp posterior border of the ulna along the entire medial forearm — the FDP lies just anterior and lateral to this border)
DIP Joints of Fingers (ask the patient to flex the DIP joints while you stabilize the middle phalanx — this isolates FDP contraction and you can feel the tendon on the volar finger)
placement: Position the patient seated with the forearm supinated and resting on a flat surface.
Identify the proximal to mid-forearm on the anterior-ulnar (medial) surface.
Palpate the FDP — ask the patient to flex the DIP joints against resistance while you palpate deep in the proximal ulnar forearm, anterior to the subcutaneous ulnar border.
The FDP is the deepest of the forearm flexors, lying directly on the interosseous membrane and the anterior surface of the ulna.
Insert the needle from the medial (ulnar) side of the forearm, just anterior to the palpable subcutaneous ulnar border, at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds of the forearm.
Advance the needle 2-2.5 cm, directing it anterolaterally toward the interosseous membrane to reach the deep compartment.
Use ultrasound to confirm needle tip position in the FDP and not in the FDS (superficial) or the interosseous membrane (too deep).
Aspirate before injecting. Multiple injection sites may be needed to target individual muscle bellies for specific digits.
setup: USG is strongly recommended — the FDP is a deep muscle that is difficult to access accurately by surface anatomy alone.
The medial (ulnar) approach is preferred as the subcutaneous ulnar border provides a reliable bony landmark.
Have the patient flex the DIP joints to confirm FDP activation before marking the site.
The anterior interosseous nerve and artery run on the anterior surface of the interosseous membrane within the FDP — identify these with ultrasound.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the medial-volar mid-forearm
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the volar-ulnar forearm at the mid-forearm level.
Identify the superficial flexor layer first: the Palmaris Longus (PL) as a small superficial structure, and the FCU on the ulnar border.
The FDS appears as the broad superficial muscle layer just deep to the PL and FCU.
Deep to FDS, the FDP is a large muscle occupying the deep volar compartment — it extends from the ulnar side across toward the radius.
CRITICAL: The Median Nerve appears as a hyperechoic oval structure between the FDS and FDP — typically on the radial (lateral) side of the image.
The Ulnar Nerve appears as a small hyperechoic oval between the FCU and FDP on the ulnar (medial) side.
The Anterior Interosseous Nerve (AIN) may be visible as a tiny hyperechoic dot on the deep surface of the FDP, near the interosseous membrane.
safetyNotes: The Median Nerve lies between FDS and FDP — needle must pass through FDS to reach FDP. Always visualize the median nerve and keep the needle tip away from it.
The Ulnar Nerve is immediately adjacent to the FCU-FDP junction — identify it before injecting the ulnar portion of FDP.
The AIN runs on the interosseous membrane deep to FDP — do not advance the needle to the bone.
Use an ulnar-side approach to avoid the median nerve when targeting FDP.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Flexor digitorum profundus spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, forearm supinated. Place probe transversely on medial-volar mid-forearm. Photo showing probe on deep volar forearm, slightly ulnar.
pearls: Target for finger flexion at DIP joints — the deeper layer
Anterior interosseous nerve (motor branch of median) is vulnerable — ID on US
Often co-injected with FDS for full finger flexion pattern
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Deep muscle — confirm placement on US.

name: Flexor Pollicis Longus
landmarks: Anterior Radial Shaft (palpate the anterior surface of the radius in the mid-forearm — the FPL originates from the middle third of the anterior radial surface; the radius is palpable lateral to the flexor muscle mass)
Thenar Eminence (palpate the fleshy mound at the base of the thumb on the palmar surface — the FPL tendon courses deep here to insert on the distal phalanx of the thumb)
Radial Artery at the Wrist (palpate the radial pulse at the radial-volar wrist, just lateral to the FCR tendon — the FPL tendon lies deep and slightly ulnar to the radial artery at this level)
placement: Position the patient seated with the forearm supinated and resting flat on a support surface.
Identify the mid-forearm on the anterior (volar) and slightly radial surface.

Palpate the FPL — ask the patient to flex the thumb at the interphalangeal (IP) joint against resistance; the FPL is the only muscle that flexes the thumb IP joint. Feel for contraction in the mid-radial forearm.
The FPL lies deep in the forearm, lateral to the FDP, directly on the anterior surface of the radius and the interosseous membrane.
Insert the needle at the mid-forearm level, on the radial side, between the brachioradialis (laterally) and the FCR (medially).
Advance the needle 2-2.5 cm, directing it posteriorly toward the radius to reach the deep compartment where the FPL lies.
Use ultrasound to confirm the needle tip is in the FPL and not in the superficial flexor muscles (FCR, FDS) or the pronator teres.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: USG is strongly recommended — the FPL is a deep muscle that requires passing through superficial flexor layers.
Have the patient repeatedly flex the thumb IP joint while you confirm the muscle belly location with ultrasound.
Approach from the radial side between the brachioradialis and FCR to minimize traversal of other muscle layers.
The anterior interosseous nerve provides motor innervation to the FPL and runs along the interosseous membrane — identify it and avoid injecting near it.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the mid-volar forearm, at the junction of the middle and distal thirds
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the volar forearm with the patient supinated.
Identify the Radius as the large hyperechoic curved bony surface on the lateral (thumb) side.
The FPL is the muscle directly overlying the anterior surface of the radius — it appears as a large, red-coded (in Dr. Bursuc's overlay) hypoechoic muscle draping over the radial shaft.
Superficial to the FPL on the radial side, identify the FCR tendon/muscle as a smaller oval structure.
Lateral (ulnar side), the FDS appears superficial, with the FDP deep between FDS and the interosseous membrane.
CRITICAL: The Radial Nerve/Anterior Interosseous Nerve (AIN) may appear as a small hyperechoic dot superficial to the FPL near the radial border.
The Median Nerve appears as a honeycomb-pattern oval between the FDS and FDP on the ulnar side of the image.
safetyNotes: The Anterior Interosseous Nerve (AIN) — a pure motor branch of the median nerve — runs on the anterior surface of the interosseous membrane between FPL and FDP. Do not inject deep to FPL.
The radial artery runs superficial and lateral — use color Doppler near the radial border.
Target the mid-substance of FPL where it overlies the radius for the safest injection zone.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Flexor pollicis longus spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, forearm supinated flat. Place probe transversely on mid-volar forearm at junction of middle and distal thirds. Photo showing probe on distal volar forearm, radial side.
pearls: Target for thumb-in-palm deformity (thumb flexion component)
Located on the radial side of the deep forearm — don't confuse with FDP
Often part of a multi-muscle thumb-in-palm program with adductor pollicis
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Narrow therapeutic window — over-dosing weakens pinch grip. Start low.

name: Adductor Pollicis
landmarks: First Web Space (palpate the fleshy area between the thumb and index finger metacarpals on the dorsal hand — the adductor pollicis fills this web space; squeeze the web space between your thumb and index finger to feel the muscle bulk)

Third Metacarpal (palpate the long bone of the middle finger on the dorsal hand — the oblique head of the adductor pollicis originates from the base of the second and third metacarpals and the capitate)
Proximal Phalanx of the Thumb, Ulnar Side (palpate the base of the thumb proximal phalanx on the ulnar side — the adductor pollicis inserts here via the ulnar sesamoid bone)
placement: Position the patient with the hand resting on a flat surface, palm down (pronated), with the thumb abducted away from the palm.
Identify the first web space between the thumb and index finger metacarpals.
Palpate the adductor pollicis — ask the patient to adduct the thumb firmly against the index finger; feel the muscle contract and bulk in the web space.
The muscle has two heads: the oblique head (deeper, originating from the carpals and metacarpals) and the transverse head (more superficial, originating from the third metacarpal shaft).
Insert the needle from the dorsal aspect of the first web space, perpendicular to the plane of the hand.
Advance 0.5-1 cm — this is a thin muscle in a confined space.
Stay within the web space and do not advance toward the palmar surface where the digital neurovascular bundles run.
Aspirate before injecting. A single injection into the bulk of the web space is usually sufficient. Use a small-gauge needle (25-27G).
setup: USG is helpful but the muscle can be injected reliably using surface anatomy in the first web space.
Use a dorsal approach to avoid the palmar digital nerves and arteries.
Have the patient adduct the thumb to confirm the muscle belly before marking.
Differentiate from the first dorsal interosseous, which lies dorsal and lateral in the web space — the adductor pollicis is deeper and more palmar.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz); a hockey-stick probe is helpful in the tight first web space
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the first web space from the dorsal approach
viewSteps: Identify the subcutaneous fat and thin dorsal skin as the most superficial layers.
The first dorsal interosseous appears as a superficial hypoechoic muscle on the dorsal-radial aspect of the web space.
Deep to the first dorsal interosseous, the adductor pollicis appears as a hypoechoic muscle filling the space between the first and second metacarpals on the palmar side.
The first and second metacarpals appear as two bright hyperechoic cortical lines on either side of the web space.
The deep palmar arch and digital arteries may be seen with color Doppler on the palmar surface of the adductor pollicis.
safetyNotes: The deep branch of the ulnar nerve passes through the adductor pollicis (between the two heads) — use a superficial injection into the transverse head when possible.
The radialis indicis artery and princeps pollicis artery run along the borders of the web space — stay in the central bulk of the muscle.
The palmar digital nerves run on the palmar aspect — use a dorsal approach and do not advance through the full depth of the web space.

probePlacementHint: Patient hand pronated on table. Place small linear or hockey-stick probe transversely across first web space from dorsal approach. Photo from above showing probe on dorsal thumb web space.
pearls: Key target for thumb-in-palm deformity (thumb adduction component)
Very small — use hockey-stick probe and minimal doses (10-20U Botox)
Dorsal approach is safer — avoids thenar neurovascular structures
dosageNote: Very small muscle — low doses only. Dorsal approach preferred.

name: Subscapularis
landmarks: Coracoid Process (palpate below the lateral clavicle, approximately 2 cm inferior and medial to the anterolateral acromion — feel for a bony prominence deep to the anterior deltoid; the subscapularis tendon passes just lateral to the coracoid)

Anterior Axillary Fold (grasp the fold of skin and muscle at the front of the axilla — the subscapularis forms the posterior wall of the axilla and its tendon contributes to the anterior fold deep to the pectoralis major)

Lesser Tuberosity of the Humerus (with the arm externally rotated, palpate the anterior shoulder just lateral to the coracoid and medial to the bicipital groove — the lesser tuberosity is where the subscapularis tendon inserts)
placement: Position the patient supine or seated with the arm abducted to 45-60 degrees and externally rotated, resting on a support.
Identify the anterior axillary fold and the coracoid process.
The subscapularis lies on the anterior (costal) surface of the scapula, deep to the serratus anterior and chest wall musculature.
Approach from the anterior axillary fold: palpate the lateral border of the scapula through the axilla.
Insert the needle through the ANTERIOR axillary fold (formed by pec major), directing it posterolaterally toward the anterior (costal) surface of the scapula.
Advance under ultrasound guidance until the needle tip is within the subscapularis muscle belly on the costal (anterior) surface of the scapula.
This is a deep injection — typically 3-4 cm depending on body habitus.
Aspirate before injecting. Multiple injection sites along the muscle belly may be needed for adequate distribution.
setup: USG is MANDATORY — the subscapularis is deep and bordered by critical neurovascular structures.
Position the patient to maximize access to the axilla and the anterior scapular surface.
Externally rotate the arm to bring the subscapularis into a more accessible position.
The axillary neurovascular bundle (axillary artery, vein, and brachial plexus cords) runs through the axilla — always identify these with Doppler before injecting.

ultrasound.probe: Low-to-medium frequency curvilinear (5-8 MHz) or high-frequency linear (10-15 MHz) in thin patients
ultrasound.orientation: Parasagittal in the anterior axilla, with the probe face directed toward the costal surface of the scapula
viewSteps: Identify the subcutaneous fat and the pectoralis major as the most superficial muscular layer on the anterior chest.
Deep to the pectoralis major, the pectoralis minor appears as a smaller triangular hypoechoic muscle.
In the axillary approach, identify the hyperechoic cortex of the scapula as your deep landmark — the subscapularis lies directly on its anterior (costal) surface.
The subscapularis appears as a large multipennate hypoechoic muscle with prominent intramuscular tendons (giving it a striated appearance) on the anterior scapular surface.
Use color Doppler to identify the axillary artery and vein, which lie superficial and lateral to the subscapularis in the axilla.
The thoracodorsal and subscapular arteries may branch near the lateral scapular border — identify them with Doppler.
safetyNotes: The axillary artery, vein, and brachial plexus cords are immediately anterior and lateral to the subscapularis in the axilla — always identify them with Doppler and ensure the needle path avoids them.
The subscapular nerve (upper and lower) innervates the subscapularis and runs on its posterior surface — stay within the muscle belly.
Do not advance the needle past the scapula, as the pleura and lung lie on the deep (costal) side of the scapula medially.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc - Subscapularis (Spasticity)

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, arm abducted 45-60° and externally rotated. Place probe parasagitally in anterior axilla. Photo from front showing probe in axillary fold aimed toward scapula.
pearls: Most important muscle for internal rotation spasticity at the shoulder
Deep in the axilla — requires careful US guidance due to proximity to axillary vessels
Inject through the anterior axillary approach with the arm abducted and externally rotated
supplies: 22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)
dosageNote: Distribute across multiple sites along the muscle belly. Deep injection — confirm tip placement before each.

name: Infraspinatus
pattern: ⚠️ NOT TYPICALLY INJECTED — External rotator (reference only)
landmarks: Spine of the Scapula (palpate the prominent bony ridge running horizontally across the posterior scapula — run your fingers from the medial scapular border laterally to the acromion; the infraspinatus fossa lies below this ridge)

Medial Border of the Scapula (palpate the vertical bony edge of the scapula medially — ask the patient to retract the scapula to make it more prominent; the infraspinatus fills the fossa between this border and the lateral border)

Greater Tuberosity of the Humerus (palpate the lateral shoulder just below the acromion with the arm in neutral rotation — the infraspinatus tendon inserts on the middle facet of the greater tuberosity)
placement: Position the patient seated, leaning forward, or in a prone position with the arms at the sides.
Identify the infraspinous fossa — the large triangular area on the posterior scapula below the spine of the scapula.
Palpate the infraspinatus muscle belly — ask the patient to externally rotate the arm against resistance while you feel for contraction in the infraspinous fossa.
The bulk of the muscle belly is located in the center of the infraspinous fossa, approximately 2-3 cm below the spine of the scapula.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the center of the infraspinous fossa, targeting the thickest part of the muscle belly.
Advance 1.5-2 cm — the muscle lies directly on the scapular bone, so you will feel resistance when the needle contacts the scapula.
The scapula acts as a protective backstop — you cannot advance past it.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute injections across the muscle belly if treating a large area of spasticity.
setup: Surface anatomy is often sufficient given the well-defined bony borders of the infraspinous fossa.
USG can confirm needle placement within the infraspinatus and not the overlying trapezius or deltoid.
Have the patient externally rotate the shoulder against resistance to confirm the muscle belly.
The suprascapular nerve and artery pass through the spinoglenoid notch at the lateral base of the spine of the scapula — avoid injecting near this area.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the infraspinous fossa, parallel to the spine of the scapula
viewSteps: Identify the subcutaneous fat and the thin trapezius muscle as the most superficial layers (the trapezius overlies the infraspinatus medially and superiorly).
The infraspinatus appears as a large hypoechoic muscle with a multipennate architecture, filling the infraspinous fossa.
The scapular bone appears as a bright hyperechoic line deep to the muscle, with posterior acoustic shadowing — this is your depth limit.
The deltoid muscle overlies the infraspinatus laterally — identify the fascial plane separating them.
At the lateral edge of the scapula near the spinoglenoid notch, use color Doppler to identify the suprascapular artery.
safetyNotes: The suprascapular nerve passes through the spinoglenoid notch at the lateral base of the scapular spine — avoid injecting within 2 cm of this landmark.
The suprascapular artery accompanies the nerve — use Doppler to identify it laterally.
The scapula provides a bony backstop, but be aware that the scapula is thin medially — in cachectic patients, use ultrasound to confirm adequate bone thickness.

probePlacementHint: Patient seated leaning forward or prone. Place probe transversely across infraspinous fossa, parallel to scapular spine. Photo from behind showing probe below scapular spine.
pearls: ⚠️ DO NOT INJECT in typical UMN spasticity. The infraspinatus is a shoulder EXTERNAL rotator. In hemiplegic shoulder posturing, the deformity is INTERNAL rotation — injecting infraspinatus would weaken the external rotators and WORSEN the pattern.
Listed here for anatomic reference only. In the rare case of post-stroke external-rotation dystonia or focal dystonia, expert consultation is required before injecting.
Suprascapular nerve runs through the spinoglenoid notch nearby — additional reason to avoid this muscle without strong indication.
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Teres Major
landmarks: Inferior Angle of the Scapula (palpate the lowest point of the scapula posteriorly — ask the patient to retract the scapula to make it prominent; the teres major originates from the posterior surface near the inferior angle and the lower lateral border)

Posterior Axillary Fold (grasp the fold of tissue at the back of the axilla — the teres major and latissimus dorsi form this fold; the teres major is the more superior and lateral of the two muscles in this fold)

Intertubercular (Bicipital) Groove of the Humerus (palpate the anterior shoulder between the greater and lesser tuberosities — the teres major inserts on the medial lip of the bicipital groove, along with the latissimus dorsi)
placement: Position the patient seated or prone with the arm adducted at the side.
Identify the posterior axillary fold and the inferior angle of the scapula.

Palpate the teres major — ask the patient to internally rotate and adduct the arm against resistance; the teres major contracts in the posterior axillary fold, superior and lateral to the latissimus dorsi.
The teres major lies along the lateral border of the scapula, running from the inferior angle to the humerus.
Insert the needle into the bulk of the muscle belly at the level of the inferior scapular angle, approximately 2-3 cm lateral to the lateral border of the scapula.
Advance 1.5-2 cm perpendicular to the skin.
Distinguish from the latissimus dorsi, which wraps around the teres major inferiorly — the teres major has a rounder, shorter belly.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: USG is recommended to distinguish teres major from the latissimus dorsi and teres minor, which are in close proximity.
Have the patient internally rotate the arm against resistance to identify the muscle belly in the posterior axillary fold.
The latissimus dorsi wraps around and lies superficial to the teres major at the posterior axillary fold — ultrasound helps separate the two.
The circumflex scapular artery passes through the triangular space (bordered by teres minor, teres major, and long head of triceps) — identify this with Doppler.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz) or curvilinear for deeper patients
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the posterior axillary fold, with the patient's arm slightly abducted
viewSteps: Position the patient seated or prone with the arm slightly abducted. Place the probe on the posterior axillary fold.
The Teres Major appears as a flat, broad hypoechoic muscle at the inferior aspect of the posterior axillary fold — in Dr. Bursuc's video it is highlighted with a purple overlay.
The Latissimus Dorsi tendon wraps around and interdigitates with the Teres Major at this level — they may appear as adjacent muscle layers.
Deep to these muscles, the scapular bone and infraspinous fossa may be partially visible.
To confirm: ask the patient to internally rotate and adduct the arm against resistance — the Teres Major will contract.
Note: the video annotation labels 'LAT D' (Latissimus Dorsi) adjacent to the Teres Major.
safetyNotes: The circumflex scapular artery and thoracodorsal neurovascular bundle run near the Teres Major — use color Doppler.
The axillary nerve is superior to the Teres Major — avoid angling the needle superiorly.
The long head of the triceps is adjacent inferiorly — confirm your target with dynamic scanning.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Teres Major Spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated or prone, arm at side. Place probe transversely over posterior axillary fold. Photo from behind showing probe on posterior axilla/inferior scapular angle.
pearls: Works synergistically with lat dorsi and subscapularis for shoulder IR/adduction
Often the forgotten muscle in shoulder internal rotation pattern — always assess it
The posterior axillary fold contains both lat dorsi and teres major — differentiate on US
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Large rotator. Distribute across 1-2 sites.

name: Adductor Longus
landmarks: Pubic Tubercle (palpate the bony prominence on the superior pubic ramus just lateral to the pubic symphysis — the adductor longus tendon originates here as a prominent, palpable cord)
Medial Femoral Condyle (palpate the large bony prominence on the inner aspect of the distal femur — the adductor longus inserts along the middle third of the linea aspera proximal to this)

Adductor Longus Tendon (with the hip slightly abducted and flexed, palpate the most prominent tendon in the proximal medial thigh originating from the pubic tubercle — this is the superficial border of the muscle)
placement: Position the patient supine with the hip slightly abducted and externally rotated, and the knee flexed with the sole of the foot resting against the opposite knee (frog-leg position).
Identify the adductor longus tendon — it is the most prominent and superficial tendon in the proximal medial thigh, easily palpable as a taut cord originating from the pubic tubercle.
The injection site is approximately 3-5 cm distal to the pubic tubercle along the muscle belly, in the medial thigh.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the muscle belly just lateral to the palpable tendon.
Advance approximately 1.5-2.5 cm — the adductor longus is the most superficial muscle in the medial compartment at this level.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2 sites along the proximal muscle belly.
setup: USG is strongly recommended to avoid the femoral neurovascular bundle, which lies lateral to the adductor longus in the femoral triangle.
Have the patient adduct the hip against resistance to confirm the correct muscle belly before injection.
The profunda femoris artery runs deep to the adductor longus — do not advance the needle too deeply.
The obturator nerve anterior branch runs on the superficial surface of the adductor brevis, deep to the adductor longus — ultrasound helps avoid it.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the proximal medial thigh, 3-5 cm distal to the pubic tubercle
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and the fascia lata as the superficial layers.
The Adductor Longus appears as a triangular or oval hypoechoic muscle belly immediately deep to the fascia — it is the most superficial adductor at this level.
Deep to the Adductor Longus, identify a bright hyperechoic fascial plane separating it from the Adductor Brevis.
The Adductor Brevis appears as a second hypoechoic muscle layer deep to the longus.
Deep to the Adductor Brevis, identify the Adductor Magnus as the largest and deepest muscle in this compartment.
Laterally, identify the femoral vessels (artery pulsating, vein compressible) — ensure the injection stays medial to these structures.
safetyNotes: The femoral artery and vein lie lateral to the adductor longus in the femoral triangle — always confirm their location with color Doppler before injecting.
The profunda femoris artery courses deep to the adductor longus — avoid deep needle advancement beyond the muscle belly.
The anterior branch of the obturator nerve runs between the adductor longus and adductor brevis — stay within the substance of the adductor longus.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, hip slightly abducted and externally rotated. Place probe transversely on proximal medial thigh, 3-5 cm below pubic tubercle. Photo showing probe on inner proximal thigh.
pearls: Most superficial adductor — the 'go-to' for adductor spasticity injection
Palpable as the prominent tendon in the proximal medial thigh with hip abduction
Femoral vessels lie LATERAL to the adductor longus in the femoral triangle — identify them with color Doppler and keep the needle medial to these structures.
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Adductor Magnus
landmarks: Ischial Tuberosity (palpate the large bony prominence at the inferior aspect of the pelvis in the buttock crease — the hamstring portion of the adductor magnus originates here)
Inferior Pubic Ramus (palpate the bony ridge extending inferolaterally from the pubic symphysis — the adductor portion of the muscle originates along this ridge)

Adductor Tubercle (palpate the small bony prominence on the medial supracondylar ridge of the distal femur just above the medial epicondyle — the hamstring portion of the adductor magnus inserts here)
placement: Position the patient prone with the legs slightly apart, or supine with the hip abducted and externally rotated.
Identify the adductor magnus — it is the deepest and largest muscle of the medial thigh compartment, best accessed from the posterior-medial approach.
The injection site is at the junction of the middle and distal thirds of the medial thigh, posterior to the gracilis and adductor longus.
Insert the needle from the posteromedial aspect of the thigh, angling slightly anteriorly.
Advance approximately 2.5-4 cm — the adductor magnus is a deep muscle and the needle must traverse the more superficial adductors or approach from the posterior aspect.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2-3 sites within the muscle belly due to its large size.
setup: USG guidance is strongly recommended due to the depth of this muscle and the proximity of the sciatic nerve and profunda femoris vessels.
Have the patient adduct the hip forcefully against resistance to help identify the muscle bulk.
The sciatic nerve runs lateral and posterior to the adductor magnus in the posterior thigh — confirm its location with ultrasound.
The adductor hiatus (gap in the distal adductor magnus) transmits the femoral vessels into the popliteal fossa — avoid injecting near the distal muscle.

ultrasound.probe: Low-frequency curvilinear (5-8 MHz) or high-frequency linear (10-15 MHz) for thinner patients
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the posteromedial thigh at the junction of the middle and distal thirds
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and the deep fascia as the superficial layers.
The Gracilis and Adductor Longus appear as superficial hypoechoic muscles in the medial compartment.
Deep to these, identify the Adductor Brevis as an intermediate hypoechoic muscle layer.
The Adductor Magnus appears as the largest and deepest hypoechoic muscle mass, lying against the posterior surface of the femur.
Identify the femur as a hyperechoic curved line with acoustic shadowing deep to the adductor magnus.
Locate the profunda femoris vessels between the adductor muscles using color Doppler — they course between the adductor longus and adductor magnus.
safetyNotes: The profunda femoris artery and vein run between the adductor longus and adductor magnus — use color Doppler to identify and avoid these vessels.
The sciatic nerve lies posterolateral to the adductor magnus in the posterior compartment — do not direct the needle too far laterally or posteriorly.
The femoral artery transitions to the popliteal artery through the adductor hiatus distally — avoid injecting in the distal third of the muscle.
The posterior branch of the obturator nerve runs on the anterior surface of the adductor magnus — ultrasound helps minimize nerve injury.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, legs slightly apart. Place probe transversely on posteromedial thigh at junction of middle and distal thirds. Photo from behind showing probe on posteromedial distal thigh.
pearls: Largest adductor — requires higher doses and multiple injection sites
Deep position makes US guidance essential
Sciatic nerve runs posterior — inject from medial approach to stay safe
supplies: Ultrasound machine with curvilinear probe (5-8 MHz, for deep muscles)
22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)

name: Biceps Femoris
landmarks: Ischial Tuberosity (palpate the large bony prominence deep in the inferior gluteal fold — the long head of the biceps femoris originates here, sharing a conjoined tendon with the semitendinosus)
Fibular Head (palpate the small bony prominence on the lateral aspect of the proximal leg just below the knee — the biceps femoris tendon inserts here and is easily palpable as a taut cord)

Lateral Hamstring Tendon (with the knee flexed to 90 degrees, palpate the prominent tendon on the posterolateral aspect of the knee running toward the fibular head — this defines the lateral border of the popliteal fossa)
placement: Position the patient prone with the knee slightly flexed over a pillow or bolster.
Identify the biceps femoris — it is the lateral hamstring muscle; have the patient flex the knee against resistance to make the muscle belly and distal tendon prominent.
The injection site for the long head is at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds of the posterior thigh on the lateral side.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the lateral muscle belly of the posterior thigh.
Advance approximately 1.5-3 cm depending on subcutaneous tissue thickness — the biceps femoris long head is relatively superficial in the lateral posterior thigh.

Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2 sites within the long head muscle belly. If the short head is also targeted, inject separately at the distal third of the lateral posterior thigh.
setup: USG is recommended to differentiate the long head from the short head and to identify the sciatic nerve.
Have the patient flex the knee against resistance to clearly delineate the biceps femoris from the medial hamstrings (semimembranosus and semitendinosus).
The sciatic nerve (or its divisions: tibial and common peroneal nerves) runs medial and deep to the biceps femoris in the posterior thigh — confirm its position before injecting.
The common peroneal nerve wraps around the fibular head immediately deep to the biceps femoris tendon distally — do not inject near the distal tendon insertion.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz) or low-frequency curvilinear (5-8 MHz) for deeper targets
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the lateral posterior thigh at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and the deep fascia (fascia lata) as the superficial layers.
The Biceps Femoris Long Head appears as a large hypoechoic muscle belly in the lateral posterior thigh, superficial to the short head.
Deep and slightly medial, identify the Biceps Femoris Short Head as a smaller hypoechoic muscle arising from the linea aspera of the femur.
Medial to the biceps femoris, identify the Semitendinosus (with its characteristic round tendon) and the Semimembranosus (broader and deeper).
Between the medial and lateral hamstrings, identify the sciatic nerve as a hyperechoic oval or honeycomb structure — it lies deep in the fascial plane.
The femur appears as a hyperechoic line with acoustic shadowing in the deep field.
safetyNotes: The sciatic nerve courses medial and deep to the biceps femoris long head — always identify it with ultrasound before injecting.
The common peroneal nerve separates from the tibial nerve in the distal thigh and runs along the medial border of the biceps femoris tendon — avoid the distal tendon region.
The popliteal artery and vein lie deep in the popliteal fossa medially — do not inject too medially or too deeply in the distal thigh.
Differentiate the long head (superficial, originates from ischial tuberosity) from the short head (deep, originates from the femur) as they may require separate targeting.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, knee flexed over pillow. Place probe transversely on lateral posterior thigh at junction of proximal and middle thirds. Photo from behind showing probe on posterolateral thigh.
pearls: Lateral hamstring — key target for lateral knee flexion patterns
Common peroneal nerve branches from sciatic near the biceps femoris tendon — identify on US
Long and short heads may need separate injection points
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Iliopsoas
landmarks: Anterior Superior Iliac Spine (ASIS) (palpate the bony projection at the anterior end of the iliac crest — the iliopsoas passes just medial to this landmark as it descends toward the hip)
Inguinal Ligament (palpate the firm band running from the ASIS to the pubic tubercle — the iliopsoas passes deep to this structure as it exits the pelvis)

Lesser Trochanter (not directly palpable, but located on the posteromedial aspect of the proximal femur approximately 5 cm below the inguinal ligament — this is the insertion point of the iliopsoas tendon)
placement: Position the patient supine with the hip in neutral position and the knee extended.
Identify the femoral artery pulse just below the inguinal ligament — the iliopsoas lies immediately lateral to the femoral neurovascular bundle.
The injection site is approximately 2-3 cm distal to the inguinal ligament, lateral to the femoral artery pulsation.
USG guidance is mandatory for this injection due to the proximity of the femoral neurovascular bundle.
Under ultrasound guidance, insert the needle lateral to the femoral vessels and advance toward the iliopsoas muscle belly.
Advance approximately 2-4 cm to reach the muscle belly deep to the sartorius and rectus femoris. Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 1-2 sites.
setup: USG guidance is mandatory — blind injection of the iliopsoas carries significant risk of femoral nerve or vascular injury.
Have the patient flex the hip against resistance to confirm the correct muscle on ultrasound (the iliopsoas will be seen contracting).

The femoral nerve sits in the fascial groove BETWEEN the iliacus and psoas major (not medial to iliopsoas) — it lies on the anterior surface of the iliacus, deep to the iliopsoas fascia, and lateral to the femoral artery. It must be identified and avoided.
The femoral artery and vein lie medial to the iliopsoas — always confirm their location with color Doppler before inserting the needle.

ultrasound.probe: Low-frequency curvilinear (5-8 MHz) or high-frequency linear (10-15 MHz) in thin patients
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the proximal anterior thigh, 2-3 cm distal to the inguinal ligament, lateral to the femoral vessels
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat, the fascia lata, and the Sartorius muscle as the most superficial structures laterally.
Medially, identify the Femoral Artery (pulsating) and Femoral Vein (compressible) within the femoral triangle.

Lateral to the femoral vessels, identify the Femoral Nerve as a hyperechoic triangular/oval structure sitting in the groove BETWEEN psoas major (medial) and iliacus (lateral), on the anterior iliacus surface.
Deep to the Sartorius and lateral to the femoral vessels, the Iliopsoas appears as a large oval hypoechoic muscle with a central hyperechoic tendon.
The Iliacus component lies laterally and the Psoas Major lies medially — the femoral nerve runs in the cleft between them. Direct the needle into the muscle belly AWAY from this groove.
Deep to the iliopsoas, identify the anterior acetabular rim or femoral head as a hyperechoic curved line.
safetyNotes: The femoral artery and vein lie medial to the iliopsoas — always use color Doppler to map the vessels before needle insertion.

CRITICAL: The femoral nerve runs WITHIN the iliopsoas complex, in the groove between iliacus (lateral) and psoas major (medial). It is NOT simply medial to iliopsoas. Identify it as a hyperechoic honeycomb structure and direct the needle to the muscle belly away from this cleft.
The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve runs near the ASIS and may cross the field — note its position if visible.
Do not inject too medially — this risks entering the femoral triangle and injuring the neurovascular bundle.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, hip neutral. Place curvilinear probe transversely on proximal anterior thigh, 2-3 cm below inguinal ligament, lateral to femoral vessels. Photo from front showing probe in inguinal/proximal thigh region.
pearls: Primary hip flexor — the most important muscle for hip flexion spasticity
Deep target requiring curvilinear probe and careful technique
Femoral nerve, artery, and vein are right next door — always identify with color Doppler
supplies: Ultrasound machine with curvilinear probe (5-8 MHz, for deep muscles)
22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)

name: Peroneus Longus
landmarks: Fibular Head (palpate the small bony prominence on the lateral aspect of the proximal leg just below the lateral knee joint line — the peroneus longus originates from the upper two-thirds of the lateral fibular surface just distal to this)
Lateral Malleolus (palpate the prominent bony landmark on the lateral ankle — the peroneus longus tendon courses posterior to the lateral malleolus in the retromalleolar groove)
