Common Peroneal Nerve at Fibular Neck (palpate the fibular neck just distal to the fibular head — the common peroneal nerve wraps around here and is at risk during proximal injections)
placement: Position the patient supine with the leg internally rotated to expose the lateral compartment, or in a lateral decubitus position with the affected side up.
Identify the peroneus longus — it is the more superficial and posterior of the two peroneal muscles in the lateral leg compartment.
The injection site is approximately 5-8 cm distal to the fibular head along the lateral aspect of the leg.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the lateral muscle belly, aiming toward the fibula.
Advance approximately 1-2 cm — the peroneus longus is relatively superficial in the lateral compartment.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2 sites along the proximal muscle belly, staying at least 3-4 cm distal to the fibular head to avoid the common peroneal nerve.
setup: USG is recommended to differentiate the peroneus longus from the peroneus brevis and to identify the common peroneal nerve.
Have the patient evert the ankle against resistance to confirm the correct muscle before injection.
The common peroneal nerve wraps around the fibular neck superficially — injections must stay at least 3-4 cm distal to the fibular head.
The superficial peroneal nerve runs between the peroneus longus and peroneus brevis — ultrasound helps avoid it.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the lateral proximal leg, 5-8 cm distal to the fibular head
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and the crural fascia as the superficial layers.
The Peroneus Longus appears as a hypoechoic muscle belly superficial and posterior in the lateral compartment, lying directly on the lateral fibular surface.
Deep and slightly anterior to the Peroneus Longus, identify the Peroneus Brevis as a smaller hypoechoic muscle also on the fibular surface.
The Fibula appears as a hyperechoic curved line with acoustic shadowing, forming the deep border of the lateral compartment.
Anteriorly, identify the anterior intermuscular septum separating the lateral compartment from the anterior compartment (extensor digitorum longus and tibialis anterior).
The Superficial Peroneal Nerve may be visible as a small hyperechoic structure between or within the peroneal muscles.
safetyNotes: The common peroneal nerve wraps around the fibular neck just distal to the fibular head — never inject within 3 cm of the fibular head.
The superficial peroneal nerve courses between the peroneus longus and brevis — identify it on ultrasound and avoid direct injection.
The anterior tibial artery passes through the interosseous membrane anteriorly — do not advance the needle too deeply through the anterior intermuscular septum.
Differentiate from the Peroneus Brevis (deeper and more anterior) to ensure injection into the correct muscle.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, leg internally rotated. Place probe transversely on lateral proximal leg, 5-8 cm below fibular head. Photo from lateral view showing probe on lateral upper calf.
pearls: ⚠️ This muscle is an EVERTOR — injecting it in equinovarus (the most common spastic foot pattern) will WORSEN the varus deformity. Only inject peroneus longus when clinical exam confirms spastic EVERSION contributing to the deformity (equinovalgus or equinus with eversion component).
Key target for foot eversion if spastic (less common than invertors)
Common peroneal nerve wraps around the fibular neck just proximal — stay distal
Superficial position makes it easy to inject once correctly identified
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Extensor Digitorum Longus
landmarks: Lateral Tibial Condyle (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral aspect of the proximal tibia just below the knee joint line — the EDL originates from the lateral condyle and the proximal anterior fibular surface)
Tibialis Anterior Tendon (palpate the prominent tendon on the anterior ankle during dorsiflexion — the EDL tendons lie just lateral to this, fanning out to the lateral four toes)
Anterior Border of the Fibula (palpate the fibula along the anterolateral leg — the EDL lies in the anterior compartment just medial to this border, between the tibialis anterior and peroneus longus)
placement: Position the patient supine with the leg in neutral position and the foot relaxed.
Identify the extensor digitorum longus — it lies in the anterior compartment of the leg, lateral to the tibialis anterior and medial to the peroneus longus.

The injection site is at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds of the anterolateral leg, approximately 8-12 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity and lateral to the tibialis anterior muscle belly.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the anterolateral muscle belly.
Advance approximately 1.5-2.5 cm — the EDL lies deep to the tibialis anterior proximally but becomes more superficial in the mid-leg.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2 sites along the muscle belly.
setup: USG is recommended to differentiate the EDL from the tibialis anterior and to identify the deep peroneal nerve and anterior tibial vessels.
Have the patient extend the toes against resistance to confirm the correct muscle before injection.
The deep peroneal nerve and anterior tibial artery run on the interosseous membrane between the tibialis anterior and EDL — avoid this neurovascular bundle.
In the proximal leg, the EDL lies partly deep to the tibialis anterior — ultrasound helps ensure accurate targeting.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the anterolateral proximal leg, at the junction of the proximal and middle thirds
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and the crural fascia as the superficial layers.
The Tibialis Anterior appears as a large hypoechoic muscle in the anteromedial compartment, lying directly on the lateral tibial surface and the interosseous membrane.
Lateral to the Tibialis Anterior, the Extensor Digitorum Longus appears as a smaller hypoechoic muscle lying on the anterior surface of the fibula and the interosseous membrane.
Between the Tibialis Anterior and the EDL, identify the Deep Peroneal Nerve and Anterior Tibial Artery as small hyperechoic and pulsatile structures respectively on the interosseous membrane.
Lateral to the EDL, identify the anterior intermuscular septum and the Peroneus Longus in the lateral compartment.
Deep to the muscles, identify the Interosseous Membrane as a bright hyperechoic line connecting the tibia and fibula.
safetyNotes: The deep peroneal nerve and anterior tibial artery lie on the interosseous membrane between the tibialis anterior and EDL — use color Doppler to identify the artery and avoid this intermuscular plane.
The superficial peroneal nerve runs in the lateral compartment and may cross into the anterior compartment distally — stay within the EDL muscle substance.
Do not advance the needle onto the interosseous membrane, as the neurovascular bundle lies here.
Differentiate from the Tibialis Anterior (more medial, larger) and the Extensor Hallucis Longus (deeper and more distal).

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, leg neutral. Place probe transversely on anterolateral proximal leg at proximal-middle third junction. Photo from anterolateral view showing probe on outer upper shin.
pearls: Target for toe extension (toe dorsiflexion) if spastic
Anterior compartment muscle — deep peroneal nerve runs between it and tibialis anterior
Rarely injected in isolation — usually part of a broader pattern
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Sternocleidomastoid (SCM)
pattern: Head rotation to opposite side, ipsilateral lateral flexion
landmarks: Sternal Head Origin (palpate the superior aspect of the manubrium sterni — the sternal head attaches as a rounded tendon just lateral to the suprasternal notch; ask the patient to turn their head to the opposite side to make it prominent)

Clavicular Head Origin (palpate the medial third of the clavicle — the clavicular head arises as a broad muscular attachment just lateral to the sternal head; the two heads form a visible triangular gap superiorly)

Mastoid Process Insertion (palpate behind the ear — feel for the prominent bony bump at the base of the skull; the SCM inserts onto the lateral surface of the mastoid process and the superior nuchal line)
placement: Position the patient supine with the head turned slightly to the contralateral side to make the SCM prominent but not maximally stretched.
Identify the sternal and clavicular heads at their origin — ask the patient to lift their head slightly off the table to confirm the muscle belly.

Palpate the muscle belly at the mid-cervical level (approximately the level of the thyroid cartilage). Grasp the muscle between thumb and index finger and LIFT it anteriorly off the carotid sheath — this 'pinch technique' physically separates the SCM from the underlying carotid artery and internal jugular vein during needle insertion.

For the sternal head: insert the needle into the mid-belly approximately 3-4 cm above the sternal origin, directing it tangentially (nearly parallel to the skin at 20-30 degrees) into the muscle to avoid deep penetration toward the carotid sheath.
Advance the needle only 0.5-1 cm into the muscle — the SCM is a relatively thin, superficial muscle and deep penetration risks entering the carotid sheath.
For the clavicular head: inject 2-3 cm above the clavicular origin, again staying superficial within the muscle belly.
Aspirate before every injection to confirm you are not intravascular — the internal jugular vein and carotid artery lie immediately deep to the SCM.
Distribute the total dose across 2-4 sites along the length of the muscle belly. Use the lowest effective dose to minimize dysphagia risk, particularly with bilateral injections.
setup: Ultrasound (USG) guidance is strongly recommended for SCM injection to avoid the carotid sheath.
Visualize the SCM as the superficial strap-like muscle overlying the carotid sheath and scalene muscles.
Use a high-frequency linear probe placed transversely across the neck at the mid-cervical level.
WARNING: Bilateral SCM injections carry a significant risk of dysphagia and neck weakness — use the minimum effective dose and consider staggering bilateral injections by 2 weeks.
When bilateral injection is necessary, preferentially dose the clavicular head over the sternal head — the sternal head is deeper and closer to pharyngeal muscles, carrying higher dysphagia risk.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the lateral neck at the level of the thyroid cartilage, perpendicular to the SCM muscle belly
viewSteps: Identify the skin and subcutaneous fat as the most superficial hyperechoic layer.
Immediately deep to the subcutaneous tissue, the SCM appears as an oval or elliptical hypoechoic muscle with a bright fascial envelope — it is typically 1-2 cm thick at the mid-cervical level.

Deep to the SCM, identify the carotid sheath containing the common carotid artery (round, pulsatile, anechoic lumen) and the internal jugular vein (compressible, oval, anechoic lumen lateral to the artery).

Lateral and deep to the carotid sheath, the anterior and middle scalene muscles appear as hypoechoic structures separated by the brachial plexus roots (small round hypoechoic dots between the scalenes).
Confirm the thyroid gland medially as a homogeneous, hyperechoic structure relative to the surrounding muscles.
safetyNotes: The carotid artery and internal jugular vein lie immediately deep to the SCM within the carotid sheath — always visualize these structures with color Doppler before injecting and keep the needle superficial within the muscle belly.

The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) enters the deep surface of the SCM at the junction of its upper third and middle third — excessive dosing or deep injection in this zone may cause accessory nerve palsy and shoulder weakness.

Bilateral injections significantly increase dysphagia risk due to weakening of neck flexion and proximity to pharyngeal muscles — use reduced doses (typically 25-50 units per side) when treating bilaterally.
The external jugular vein crosses superficially over the SCM — use color Doppler to identify and avoid it.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided SCM BTX injection for cervical dystonia

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, head turned slightly contralateral. Place probe transversely on lateral neck at thyroid cartilage level. Photo from front/lateral showing probe across SCM muscle belly on neck.
pearls: Most commonly injected neck muscle for torticollis and laterocollis
Carotid artery and internal jugular vein are immediately deep — ALWAYS use US
The spinal accessory nerve runs through the muscle — EMG can help avoid it
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Upper Trapezius
landmarks: External Occipital Protuberance (palpate the midline of the occiput — feel for the prominent bony bump at the base of the skull posteriorly; the upper trapezius originates from the superior nuchal line extending laterally from this point)

Spinous Process of C7 (palpate the posterior midline of the neck — flex the neck forward and identify the most prominent spinous process at the cervicothoracic junction; the upper trapezius fibers originate from the nuchal ligament above this level)

Lateral Third of the Clavicle and Acromion (palpate along the clavicle laterally to its end, then continue posteriorly onto the flat bony shelf of the acromion — the upper trapezius inserts along the posterior border of the lateral clavicle and the medial margin of the acromion)
placement: Position the patient seated upright with arms relaxed at the sides, or prone with the forehead resting on hands.
Identify the upper trapezius by asking the patient to shrug their shoulders — the muscle belly will become prominent as a thick ridge between the neck and shoulder.
Palpate the thickest portion of the muscle belly, which is typically midway between the cervical spine and the acromion, overlying the supraspinous fossa.
Grasp the muscle belly between the thumb and fingers to lift it away from underlying structures and confirm its bulk.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin surface into the midpoint of the palpated muscle bulk, advancing 1-2 cm into the muscle belly.
Stay within the superficial muscle bulk — do not advance beyond 2 cm to avoid penetrating into the supraspinous fossa or toward the lung apex medially.
Aspirate before injecting to confirm you are not intravascular.
Distribute the total dose across 2-3 sites along the muscle belly, spacing injections approximately 2-3 cm apart along the line from the occiput to the acromion.
setup: Ultrasound (USG) guidance is recommended, particularly for medial injections near the cervical spine where the muscle thins and the lung apex may be at risk.
Visualize the upper trapezius as the most superficial muscle layer overlying the supraspinatus and levator scapulae.
Use a high-frequency linear probe placed transversely across the upper trapezius ridge.
When injecting the medial fibers near the cervicothoracic junction, always confirm the depth of the lung apex with ultrasound to prevent pneumothorax.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the upper trapezius ridge, midway between the cervical spinous processes and the acromion
viewSteps: Identify the skin and subcutaneous fat as the most superficial hyperechoic layer.
Deep to the fat, the upper trapezius appears as a broad, flat hypoechoic muscle with parallel fiber architecture and bright fascial borders on its superficial and deep surfaces.
Beneath the deep fascia of the trapezius, identify the supraspinatus muscle filling the supraspinous fossa (hypoechoic, with a different fiber direction) when scanning laterally over the scapula.
When scanning more medially, the levator scapulae appears deep to the trapezius as a smaller hypoechoic muscle running obliquely toward the cervical transverse processes.
Deep to the muscular layers, identify the hyperechoic bony cortex of the scapular spine or, if scanning medially, the posterior ribs and pleural line.
safetyNotes: The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) runs on the deep surface of the upper trapezius, approximately 2-3 cm above the scapular spine in the posterior triangle of the neck — avoid deep injections in this zone and use the minimum effective dose to preserve voluntary shoulder elevation.
The transverse cervical artery and suprascapular artery run deep to the trapezius — use color Doppler when injecting near the base of the neck or the scapular notch.

Medial injections near the cervicothoracic junction risk pneumothorax if the needle is advanced too deeply — always confirm the pleural line on ultrasound and stay superficial within the muscle belly.
The greater occipital nerve emerges through the upper trapezius near its occipital attachment — avoid injecting within 2 cm of the superior nuchal line to prevent occipital neuralgia.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided BTX injection (Splenius capitis, Semispinalis capitis and Levator scapula)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, arms relaxed. Place probe transversely on upper trapezius ridge, midway between cervical spine and acromion. Photo from behind showing probe on upper trap between neck and shoulder.
pearls: Very commonly injected for cervical dystonia and shoulder elevation
Superficial muscle — easy to inject but watch for spread to adjacent muscles
Spinal accessory nerve is the motor nerve — avoid injecting near the posterior triangle
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Levator Scapulae
pattern: Scapular elevation, cervical lateral flexion and rotation
landmarks: Transverse Processes of C1-C4 (palpate the lateral neck just posterior to the SCM — press gently toward the spine to feel the small bony prominences of the cervical transverse processes; the levator scapulae originates from the posterior tubercles of C1-C4)

Superior Angle of the Scapula (palpate the upper back — locate the superomedial corner of the scapula by following the medial scapular border superiorly until it curves laterally; this bony point sits deep to the trapezius approximately 2-3 cm lateral to the spinous processes at the T2 level)

Medial Border of the Scapula above the Scapular Spine (palpate along the medial scapular border from the superior angle downward to the root of the scapular spine — the levator scapulae inserts along this segment between the superior angle and the spine of the scapula)
placement: Position the patient seated upright with arms relaxed, or prone with the forehead resting on hands. Slight contralateral lateral flexion of the neck can help stretch and define the muscle.
Identify the superior angle of the scapula by palpating the superomedial corner of the scapula — this is the most reliable surface landmark for the levator scapulae insertion.

Palpate the levator scapulae deep to the upper trapezius by pressing firmly at the superior angle of the scapula — the muscle belly may be felt as a firm, tender band running superiorly toward the cervical spine.
The primary injection target is at or just above the superior angle of the scapula, where the muscle is most accessible beneath the trapezius.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin overlying the superior angle of the scapula, advancing through the trapezius (1-1.5 cm) and into the levator scapulae beneath it (an additional 0.5-1 cm).
Total needle depth is typically 2-3 cm depending on body habitus — use ultrasound to confirm the needle tip is within the levator scapulae and not beyond it.
Aspirate before injecting to confirm you are not intravascular.

Distribute the dose across 1-2 sites near the scapular insertion. Avoid injecting the upper cervical portion of the muscle without ultrasound guidance due to the proximity of the vertebral artery and nerve roots.
setup: Ultrasound (USG) guidance is essential for levator scapulae injection — this is a deep muscle that cannot be reliably isolated by palpation alone.
Visualize the levator scapulae deep to the trapezius, running from the superior angle of the scapula toward the cervical transverse processes.
Use a high-frequency linear probe placed transversely over the superior angle of the scapula to identify the muscle layers.
EMG confirmation may also be used as an adjunct — ask the patient to shrug and tilt the head ipsilaterally to activate the levator scapulae selectively.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse over the superior angle of the scapula, approximately 2-3 cm lateral to the cervicothoracic spinous processes at the T1-T2 level
viewSteps: Identify the skin and subcutaneous fat as the most superficial hyperechoic layer.
Deep to the fat, the upper trapezius appears as the first hypoechoic muscular layer with a bright superficial fascial border.

Beneath the trapezius, a thin hyperechoic fascial plane separates it from the levator scapulae, which appears as a smaller, deeper hypoechoic muscle with obliquely oriented fibers running toward the cervical spine.
The levator scapulae attaches to the hyperechoic bony cortex of the superior angle of the scapula — use this as your key landmark to confirm correct identification.

Deep and medial to the levator scapulae, the posterior scalene and the serratus posterior superior may be visible. Further deep, identify the hyperechoic pleural line and ribs with posterior acoustic shadowing.
safetyNotes: The dorsal scapular nerve runs on or through the levator scapulae along its deep surface, often accompanied by the dorsal scapular artery — use color Doppler to identify vascular structures and inject with care to minimize nerve injury.
The vertebral artery lies anterior to the cervical transverse processes — never direct the needle anteriorly or medially toward the transverse processes without ultrasound visualization.
The lung apex extends above the first rib medially — when injecting near the medial scapular border at the T1-T2 level, always confirm the pleural line on ultrasound and maintain a safe distance.

The spinal accessory nerve crosses the posterior triangle superficial to the levator scapulae — deep injection through the trapezius helps avoid this nerve, but be aware of its course if injecting the superior cervical portion of the muscle.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided BTX injection (Splenius capitis, Semispinalis capitis and Levator scapula)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, arms relaxed. Place probe transversely over superior angle of scapula, 2-3 cm lateral to C7-T2 spinous processes. Photo from behind showing probe on posterolateral neck/upper back.
pearls: Deep to upper trapezius — must visualize past the trap on US
Contributes to scapular elevation and lateral cervical flexion
Dorsal scapular nerve may be nearby — consider EMG confirmation
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Pronator Quadratus
landmarks: Distal Radius (palpate the flat bony prominence on the radial side of the distal forearm just proximal to the wrist crease — pronator quadratus lies directly over the volar distal radius and ulna)
Distal Ulna (palpate the ulnar styloid and move proximally ~4 cm — the muscle origin is the anterior distal ulnar shaft)
Flexor Tendons at the Wrist (palpate the FCR and palmaris longus tendons — pronator quadratus lies DEEP to all the flexor tendons on the volar distal forearm)
placement: Position the patient supine or seated with the forearm supinated and resting on a support, wrist in neutral.
Identify the volar (anterior) distal forearm, approximately 3-5 cm proximal to the wrist crease.
⚠️ USG is MANDATORY — the muscle is deep and the median nerve, ulnar nerve, radial artery, and ulnar artery all cross this plane.
Use a dorsal or ulnar approach to avoid the median nerve and radial/ulnar arteries on the volar side.
DORSAL APPROACH (preferred): Insert the needle from the dorsal distal forearm, between the radius and ulna, advancing through the interosseous membrane into pronator quadratus on the volar side.

Under ultrasound, track the needle tip through the interosseous membrane and stop as soon as it enters the thin hypoechoic pronator quadratus muscle belly just superficial to the pronator line of the radius.
The muscle is thin (5-10 mm) — advance only the minimum distance needed to place the tip within it.
Aspirate before injecting. A single injection site is usually sufficient.
setup: USG guidance is MANDATORY — pronator quadratus is deep and surrounded by neurovascular structures (median nerve, anterior interosseous nerve, radial/ulnar arteries).
A dorsal approach avoids the median nerve and the radial/ulnar arteries on the volar side.
Use color Doppler to map the radial and ulnar arteries before needle insertion.

The anterior interosseous nerve (branch of median nerve) runs on the volar surface of the interosseous membrane deep to FDP and FPL — it innervates pronator quadratus. Stay within the muscle belly, not the plane between muscles.
Consider EMG guidance as an adjunct to confirm placement in this small deep muscle.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the distal volar forearm, 3-5 cm proximal to the wrist crease
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the volar distal forearm, 3-5 cm proximal to the wrist crease.
Identify the radius (radial side) and ulna (ulnar side) as bright hyperechoic cortical curves with posterior shadowing — these are your deep lateral landmarks.
Between the radius and ulna on the deep volar side, identify pronator quadratus as a thin (5-10 mm) flat hypoechoic muscle band with horizontal fibers running directly between the two bones.
Superficial to pronator quadratus, identify the long flexor tendons (FDS, FDP, FPL) as hyperechoic oval cross-sections with a striated fibrillar pattern.
CRITICAL: The median nerve appears as a honeycomb hyperechoic oval between the FDS and FPL tendons, more superficial than pronator quadratus. Map it before inserting the needle.
Use color Doppler to identify the radial artery (radial side) and ulnar artery (ulnar side) — both should be out of the needle path.
To confirm muscle identification, ask the patient to pronate the forearm against resistance — pronator quadratus will contract.
safetyNotes: The median nerve and anterior interosseous nerve lie in this plane — always identify the median nerve on US before needle insertion.
The radial and ulnar arteries lie on the volar surface of the forearm — use color Doppler to map them. A dorsal approach avoids both.
The pronator quadratus is thin — minor needle over-advancement can exit the muscle. Use in-plane technique with continuous needle tip visualization.
Very low doses are appropriate given the small muscle volume — start at 10-20 U Botox per site.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, forearm supinated on table. Place probe transversely on volar distal forearm, 3-5 cm proximal to wrist crease. Photo from volar view showing probe on distal forearm.
pearls: The TRUE distal pronator — often missed because pronator teres gets all the attention
Thin, deep muscle — USG mandatory; a dorsal approach through the interosseous membrane is safest
Low doses only (10-25 U Botox) given the small muscle volume
Co-inject with pronator teres for refractory forearm pronation pattern

name: Splenius Capitis
landmarks: Spinous Processes of C3-T3 (palpate the posterior midline of the neck and upper thoracic spine — press firmly on each spinous process from C3 downward to T3; the splenius capitis originates from the lower half of the nuchal ligament and the spinous processes of C7 through T3)

Mastoid Process (palpate behind the ear — feel for the prominent bony bump at the base of the skull; the splenius capitis inserts onto the lateral surface of the mastoid process, just deep and posterior to the SCM attachment)

Superior Nuchal Line (palpate from the mastoid process medially along the occiput — the splenius capitis also inserts along the lateral portion of the superior nuchal line, just deep to the upper trapezius attachment)
placement: Position the patient seated upright with the neck slightly flexed to open the posterior cervical space, or prone with the forehead resting on hands.
Identify the posterior cervical triangle between the upper trapezius and the SCM — the splenius capitis lies deep to the upper trapezius in this region.

Palpate the posterolateral neck approximately 2-3 cm lateral to the cervical midline at the C2-C4 level — the splenius capitis forms a thick muscular band running obliquely from the lower cervical/upper thoracic spinous processes to the mastoid process.
Ask the patient to rotate the head ipsilaterally against resistance — the splenius capitis will contract and become more palpable beneath the trapezius.
The primary injection target is at the C2-C4 level, approximately 2-3 cm lateral to the midline, where the muscle belly is thickest and most accessible beneath the trapezius.

Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin, advancing through the trapezius (1-1.5 cm) and into the splenius capitis beneath it (an additional 0.5-1.5 cm). Total depth is typically 2-3.5 cm depending on body habitus.
Aspirate before injecting to confirm you are not intravascular — the vertebral artery lies deep and medial, and the occipital artery may cross the superficial surface.
Distribute the total dose across 2-3 sites along the muscle belly, spacing injections approximately 2-3 cm apart along its course from the upper cervical spine toward the mastoid.
setup: Ultrasound (USG) guidance is strongly recommended — splenius capitis lies deep to the trapezius and cannot be reliably distinguished from the underlying semispinalis capitis by palpation alone.
Visualize the splenius capitis as the intermediate muscle layer between the superficial trapezius and the deep semispinalis capitis.
Use a high-frequency linear probe placed transversely across the posterolateral neck at the C2-C4 level, 2-3 cm lateral to the spinous processes.

EMG confirmation is a useful adjunct — ask the patient to rotate the head to the ipsilateral side to selectively activate splenius capitis and confirm needle placement within the correct muscle layer.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the posterolateral neck at the C2-C4 level, approximately 2-3 cm lateral to the midline spinous processes
viewSteps: Identify the skin and subcutaneous fat as the most superficial hyperechoic layer.
Deep to the fat, the upper trapezius appears as the first hypoechoic muscular layer with a bright fascial envelope — it is relatively thin in this region (0.5-1 cm).

Beneath the trapezius, a hyperechoic fascial plane separates it from the splenius capitis, which appears as a thicker hypoechoic muscle layer (1-2 cm) with obliquely oriented fibers running from the midline inferiorly toward the mastoid superolaterally.

Deep to the splenius capitis, another fascial plane separates it from the semispinalis capitis, which is the deepest and thickest posterior cervical muscle layer appearing as a broad hypoechoic band directly overlying the cervical laminae.
Deep to the semispinalis capitis, identify the hyperechoic bony cortex of the cervical laminae and spinous processes with posterior acoustic shadowing.
Use color Doppler to identify the vertebral artery medially (within the transverse foramen) and any branches of the occipital artery superficially.
safetyNotes: The vertebral artery runs through the transverse foramina of C1-C6 — it lies deep and medial to the splenius capitis. Never direct the needle medially toward the transverse processes without continuous ultrasound visualization.

The greater occipital nerve (dorsal ramus of C2) pierces the semispinalis capitis and may traverse the splenius capitis near the superior nuchal line — avoid injecting within 2 cm of the occipital insertion to minimize risk of occipital neuralgia.
The occipital artery courses superficially over the splenius capitis near its mastoid insertion — use color Doppler to identify and avoid it when injecting the upper portion of the muscle.

The spinal accessory nerve crosses the posterior triangle superficial to the splenius capitis — it is generally protected by the overlying trapezius, but awareness of its course is important when injecting the lateral muscle belly.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided BTX injection (Splenius capitis, Semispinalis capitis and Levator scapula)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, neck slightly flexed. Place probe transversely on posterolateral neck at C2-C4 level, 2-3 cm lateral to midline. Photo from behind showing probe on posterolateral neck between midline and mastoid.
pearls: The #1 target in rotational cervical dystonia — ipsilateral head rotation is its primary action
Lies deep to trapezius but superficial to semispinalis capitis — US is critical to distinguish the correct layer
Often the highest-dose muscle in cervical dystonia treatment — 50-100 U Botox per side
Combined splenius capitis + contralateral SCM injection is the classic rotational torticollis pair
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Semispinalis Capitis
landmarks: Transverse Processes of C4-T6 (palpate the posterolateral neck and upper thoracic spine — the semispinalis capitis originates from the articular processes of C4-C6 and the transverse processes of C7 through T6; these are deep and not individually palpable but define the muscle's course)

Between the Superior and Inferior Nuchal Lines (palpate the occiput — the semispinalis capitis inserts onto the occipital bone in the depression between the superior and inferior nuchal lines, medial to the splenius capitis insertion)
External Occipital Protuberance (palpate the midline of the occiput — the semispinalis capitis inserts just lateral to this bony prominence, occupying the broad area between the nuchal lines)
placement: Position the patient seated with the neck slightly flexed to open the posterior cervical space, or prone with the forehead resting on hands.
Identify the posterior midline of the neck — the semispinalis capitis is a bilateral paramedian muscle lying on either side of the midline, deep to both the trapezius and splenius capitis.
The primary injection target is at the C2-C5 level, approximately 1-2 cm lateral to the midline, where the muscle belly is thickest.

This is a DEEP muscle — the needle must traverse the trapezius and splenius capitis to reach it. USG guidance is essential to confirm correct depth and avoid injecting into the more superficial splenius capitis.

Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin, advancing through the trapezius, then the splenius capitis, and into the semispinalis capitis beneath. Total depth is typically 3-5 cm depending on body habitus.
Aspirate before injecting to confirm you are not intravascular — the vertebral artery lies anterior to this muscle, separated by the cervical laminae.
Use CONSERVATIVE doses — the semispinalis capitis is a deep bilateral extensor, and excessive weakening can cause debilitating anterocollis (dropped head syndrome).
Distribute the dose across 1-2 sites per side, staying close to the midline at the C2-C5 level.
setup: Ultrasound (USG) guidance is essential — the semispinalis capitis is the deepest of the posterior cervical muscle layers and cannot be isolated by palpation.
Visualize the semispinalis capitis as the deepest muscular layer directly overlying the cervical laminae, beneath the splenius capitis and trapezius.
Use a high-frequency linear probe placed transversely across the posterior neck at the C2-C5 level, 1-2 cm lateral to the midline.
Use a longer needle (2-3 inch, 22-25G) to reach this deep muscle — confirm the needle tip position on ultrasound before injecting.
EMG confirmation is recommended — ask the patient to extend the neck against resistance to activate the semispinalis capitis.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the posterior midline neck at the C2-C5 level, centered 1-2 cm lateral to the spinous processes
viewSteps: Identify the skin and subcutaneous fat as the most superficial hyperechoic layer.
Deep to the fat, the trapezius appears as the first thin hypoechoic muscular layer at this paramedian location.
Beneath the trapezius, the splenius capitis appears as an intermediate hypoechoic muscle layer with obliquely oriented fibers — this is the muscle you must traverse to reach the semispinalis capitis.

Deep to the splenius capitis, a fascial plane leads to the semispinalis capitis — the thickest and deepest of the three muscle layers. It appears as a broad, hypoechoic muscle directly overlying the hyperechoic cervical laminae.

Deep to the semispinalis capitis, identify the bright hyperechoic cortex of the cervical laminae and spinous processes with posterior acoustic shadowing — this is your deep limit. Do not advance the needle beyond the muscle into the lamina.
Near the midline, the nuchal ligament appears as a hyperechoic band between the bilateral semispinalis capitis muscles.
safetyNotes: The vertebral artery lies anterior to the cervical laminae within the transverse foramina — the laminae serve as a protective bony barrier, but lateral needle angulation toward the transverse processes could risk vertebral artery injury. Always keep the needle directed perpendicular to the skin and stop at the laminar cortex.
The greater occipital nerve (dorsal ramus of C2) pierces the semispinalis capitis near the C1-C2 level — injection at this level may cause temporary occipital numbness or neuralgia.

Excessive dosing or bilateral injection can cause severe neck extensor weakness and anterocollis (dropped head syndrome) — use the minimum effective dose (25-50 U Botox per side) and monitor for neck weakness at follow-up.
The deep cervical artery (branch of the costocervical trunk) runs on the deep surface of the semispinalis capitis — use color Doppler to identify and avoid vascular structures before injecting.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided BTX injection (Splenius capitis, Semispinalis capitis and Levator scapula)

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, neck flexed. Place probe transversely on posterior neck at C2-C5 level, 1-2 cm lateral to midline. Photo from behind showing probe on posterior midline neck just below the occiput.
pearls: The primary target for retrocollis — bilateral neck extension is its chief action
Deepest of the three posterior cervical layers (trapezius > splenius capitis > semispinalis capitis) — USG is essential to reach the correct layer
Use conservative doses (25-50 U per side) — excessive weakening causes dropped head syndrome (anterocollis)
The greater occipital nerve pierces this muscle — patients may report temporary occipital numbness after injection

name: Scalenes (Anterior, Middle, Posterior)
pattern: Ipsilateral lateral neck flexion (laterocollis), accessory inspiration
landmarks: Posterior Border of the SCM (palpate the lateral neck — follow the posterior edge of the sternocleidomastoid from the mastoid downward; the scalenes lie immediately posterior and deep to the SCM in the lateral neck)

Transverse Processes of C2-C7 (palpate deeply in the lateral neck posterior to the SCM — the anterior scalene originates from the anterior tubercles of the transverse processes of C3-C6, the middle scalene from the posterior tubercles of C2-C7, and the posterior scalene from C5-C7)

First and Second Ribs (palpate above the clavicle in the supraclavicular fossa — the anterior scalene inserts onto the scalene tubercle of the first rib, the middle scalene onto the superior surface of the first rib posterior to the subclavian groove, and the posterior scalene onto the external surface of the second rib)
placement: Position the patient supine with the head in neutral position or turned slightly to the contralateral side to expose the lateral neck.
Identify the posterior border of the SCM — the scalenes lie in the posterolateral neck between the SCM anteriorly and the upper trapezius posteriorly.

CRITICAL: Ultrasound guidance is MANDATORY for scalene injection — the brachial plexus roots pass between the anterior and middle scalenes (interscalene groove), and the subclavian artery passes anterior to the anterior scalene. The phrenic nerve runs on the anterior surface of the anterior scalene.
Under ultrasound, identify the target scalene muscle and confirm the location of the brachial plexus, subclavian/vertebral vessels, and phrenic nerve before inserting the needle.

Insert the needle using an in-plane technique under continuous ultrasound visualization, directing it laterally to medially into the muscle belly while avoiding the interscalene groove and its neurovascular contents.
Aspirate before injecting to confirm you are not intravascular.
Use LOW doses — the scalenes are small muscles adjacent to critical neurovascular structures. Start at the low end of the dosing range.
Distribute the dose across 1-2 sites within the targeted scalene muscle belly. Avoid injecting near the interscalene groove or the muscle origins/insertions where nerves are concentrated.
setup: Ultrasound (USG) guidance is MANDATORY — the brachial plexus, phrenic nerve, subclavian artery, and vertebral artery are all intimately related to the scalenes.

Visualize the scalene muscles as the lateral neck muscles between the SCM and the levator scapulae/upper trapezius, with the brachial plexus roots visible as a cluster of round hypoechoic structures in the interscalene groove.
Use a high-frequency linear probe placed transversely across the lateral neck at the C5-C7 level.
Color Doppler is essential — map the vertebral artery, transverse cervical artery, and subclavian artery before needle insertion.
EMG confirmation is strongly recommended as an adjunct — ask the patient to laterally flex the neck ipsilaterally to selectively activate the scalenes.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the lateral neck at the C5-C7 level, posterior to the SCM and anterior to the upper trapezius
viewSteps: Identify the SCM as the most superficial anterolateral muscle — use it as your anterior landmark.
Posterior and deep to the SCM, the anterior scalene appears as a triangular or trapezoidal hypoechoic muscle lying directly anterior to the brachial plexus roots.

The brachial plexus nerve roots appear as a cluster of small, round, hypoechoic structures (with hyperechoic rims) in the interscalene groove between the anterior and middle scalenes — this is your critical landmark. The roots typically appear as a 'traffic light' or 'bunch of grapes' pattern at the C5-C7 level.
Posterior to the brachial plexus, the middle scalene appears as a larger hypoechoic muscle — it is typically the thickest of the three scalenes at this level.
The posterior scalene lies posterior and slightly inferior to the middle scalene, often best visualized at the C7-T1 level just above the first rib.
Deep to the scalenes, identify the hyperechoic transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae with posterior acoustic shadowing. The vertebral artery may be visible within the transverse foramina.
Use color Doppler to identify the vertebral artery (within the transverse foramen), the subclavian artery (inferior, deep to the anterior scalene), and the transverse cervical artery.
safetyNotes: The brachial plexus roots (C5-T1) pass through the interscalene groove between the anterior and middle scalenes — injection into or near the interscalene groove can cause brachial plexopathy with arm weakness and numbness. ALWAYS identify the plexus before injecting and keep the needle tip within the muscle belly, away from the groove.

The phrenic nerve (C3-C5) runs on the ANTERIOR surface of the anterior scalene — injection of the anterior scalene carries the highest risk of phrenic nerve paralysis and hemidiaphragm weakness. Use the minimum effective dose and NEVER inject the anterior scalene bilaterally in the same session.

The subclavian artery passes anterior to the anterior scalene at its inferior extent, and the vertebral artery lies within the transverse foramina deep to the scalenes — always use color Doppler to identify these vessels before injecting.

The lung apex lies immediately inferior to the scalenes behind the first rib — keep the needle directed medially to laterally (not inferiorly) to avoid pneumothorax, and always visualize the pleural line.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided scalene BTX injection for cervical dystonia

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, head neutral or slightly turned contralateral. Place probe transversely on lateral neck at C5-C7 level, posterior to SCM. Photo from front/lateral showing probe on lateral neck behind the SCM.
pearls: The primary target for laterocollis — ipsilateral lateral flexion is the chief action
USG is MANDATORY — the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels are millimeters away
The phrenic nerve runs on the anterior scalene surface — NEVER inject anterior scalenes bilaterally in the same session
Start with low doses (15-25 U Botox per muscle) and titrate up — overcorrection or nerve spread can be disabling
The middle scalene is often the safest and largest target for injection

name: Longissimus Capitis
pattern: Head and neck extension (retrocollis), ipsilateral lateral flexion and rotation
landmarks: Transverse Processes of T1-T5 (palpate the upper thoracic paraspinal region — the longissimus capitis originates from the transverse processes of the upper five thoracic vertebrae; these are deep and serve as a conceptual origin rather than a palpable landmark)
Articular Processes of C4-C7 (the muscle also receives slips from the articular processes of the lower cervical vertebrae as it courses superiorly — these are deep and not individually palpable)

Mastoid Process — Posterior Surface (palpate behind the ear and feel for the bony prominence — the longissimus capitis inserts onto the posterior margin of the mastoid process, deep to the splenius capitis and between the splenius capitis laterally and the semispinalis capitis medially)
placement: Position the patient seated with the neck slightly flexed to open the posterior cervical space, or prone with the forehead resting on hands.

Identify the posterior neck approximately 2-4 cm lateral to the midline at the C4-C7 level — the longissimus capitis lies deep in the posterior cervical musculature, between the semispinalis capitis medially and the levator scapulae laterally.
This is a DEEP muscle in the erector spinae group — the needle must traverse the trapezius and splenius capitis to reach it. USG guidance is essential.

Under ultrasound, identify the longissimus capitis as the slender muscle column lying lateral to the semispinalis capitis and medial to the levator scapulae, directly overlying the cervical articular pillars.

Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin at the C4-C7 level, approximately 2-4 cm lateral to the midline, advancing through the superficial muscle layers to reach the longissimus capitis. Total depth is typically 3-5 cm depending on body habitus.
Aspirate before injecting to confirm you are not intravascular — the vertebral artery and deep cervical vessels are in proximity.

Use CONSERVATIVE doses — the longissimus capitis is a relatively small deep muscle, and excessive dosing risks spread to adjacent semispinalis capitis and other deep extensors, worsening anterocollis.
A single injection site per side is usually sufficient given the small muscle volume.
setup: Ultrasound (USG) guidance is essential — the longissimus capitis is a deep, slender muscle that cannot be isolated by palpation and is surrounded by other deep cervical muscles.
Visualize the longissimus capitis as a small muscle column between the broader semispinalis capitis medially and the levator scapulae laterally, overlying the cervical articular pillars.
Use a high-frequency linear probe placed transversely across the posterior neck at the C4-C7 level, 2-4 cm lateral to the midline.

EMG confirmation is strongly recommended — ask the patient to extend the neck against resistance while palpating laterally to confirm activation of the longissimus capitis rather than the larger semispinalis capitis.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the posterolateral neck at the C4-C7 level, approximately 2-4 cm lateral to the midline spinous processes
viewSteps: Identify the skin and subcutaneous fat as the most superficial hyperechoic layer.
Deep to the fat, the trapezius appears as a thin hypoechoic muscular layer in this region.
Beneath the trapezius, the splenius capitis appears as an intermediate hypoechoic muscle layer — traverse this layer to reach the deep compartment.

In the deep compartment, the semispinalis capitis appears as the large medial muscle mass directly overlying the laminae. Lateral to the semispinalis capitis, the longissimus capitis appears as a smaller, distinct muscle column overlying the articular pillars (facet joints) of the cervical vertebrae.

The articular pillars appear as a series of hyperechoic bony prominences with posterior acoustic shadowing — these are your key deep landmark, lying directly beneath the longissimus capitis and lateral to the laminae.
Lateral to the longissimus capitis, the levator scapulae may be visible as a separate muscle running toward the scapula.
safetyNotes: The vertebral artery lies within the transverse foramina anterior to the articular pillars — never advance the needle beyond the bony cortex of the articular pillar. The bone serves as a natural stop.
The deep cervical artery and vein run in the deep posterior cervical musculature — use color Doppler to identify and avoid vascular structures before injecting.
The dorsal rami of the cervical spinal nerves pass through or near the longissimus capitis — injection may cause temporary local paresthesias or numbness in the posterior neck.

Excessive dosing risks weakening adjacent deep extensors (semispinalis capitis, multifidus) due to toxin spread, potentially causing or worsening anterocollis — use the minimum effective dose (25-50 U Botox per side).
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided deep posterior cervical BTX injection for cervical dystonia

probePlacementHint: Patient seated, neck slightly flexed. Place probe transversely on posterolateral neck at C4-C7 level, 2-4 cm lateral to midline. Photo from behind showing probe on posterolateral neck over the articular pillars.
pearls: Part of the erector spinae group in the neck — contributes to retrocollis and ipsilateral rotation
Deep and slender — lies between the larger semispinalis capitis medially and levator scapulae laterally
The articular pillars (facet joints) serve as the key US landmark directly beneath this muscle
Conservative dosing (25-50 U per side) — toxin spread to adjacent deep extensors can cause dropped head
Often targeted in combination with semispinalis capitis for refractory retrocollis patterns

name: Masseter
pattern: Jaw clenching, bruxism, oromandibular dystonia (jaw closing type)
landmarks: Zygomatic Arch (palpate the bony ridge extending from just anterior to the tragus of the ear forward toward the cheekbone — the superficial masseter originates from the inferior border of the zygomatic arch and its anterior two-thirds)

Angle of the Mandible (palpate the prominent bony angle at the posteroinferior corner of the jaw — the masseter inserts onto the lateral surface of the mandibular ramus and angle; ask the patient to clench their teeth to make the muscle bulk prominent)
Mandibular Ramus (palpate the flat bony surface between the zygomatic arch and the angle of the mandible — this is the lateral surface of the ramus, entirely covered by the masseter)
placement: Position the patient seated upright or supine with the jaw relaxed and slightly open.
Ask the patient to clench their teeth forcefully — the masseter will bulge prominently as a thick rectangular mass over the mandibular ramus between the zygomatic arch and the angle of the mandible.
Identify the thickest bulk of the muscle, typically at the mid-ramus level, approximately 1-2 cm anterior to the tragus and 1-2 cm superior to the mandibular angle.
Pinch the muscle belly between thumb (laterally) and fingers (intraorally or medially) to confirm its thickness and separate it from the overlying parotid gland tissue.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin surface directly into the center of the palpated muscle bulk. Advance 1-1.5 cm into the muscle belly.

CAUTION: Stay in the posterior half of the muscle to avoid the parotid duct (Stensen's duct), which crosses the mid-masseter anteriorly at approximately the level of the tragus. The duct runs from the parotid gland anteriorly to pierce the buccinator at the second upper molar.
Aspirate before injecting to confirm you are not intravascular — the facial artery and transverse facial artery run in this region.

Distribute the total dose across 3 sites in the lower two-thirds of the muscle belly, spacing injections approximately 1 cm apart. Avoid the upper third near the zygomatic arch to minimize risk to the zygomatic branch of the facial nerve.
setup: Ultrasound guidance is increasingly recommended to confirm needle placement within the masseter and to visualize the parotid gland and facial vessels.
The parotid gland overlies the posterior portion of the masseter — identify it on ultrasound as a homogeneous, hyperechoic structure superficial to or overlapping the muscle.
Use color Doppler to identify the transverse facial artery (runs between the zygomatic arch and the parotid duct) and the facial artery (crosses the mandible anteriorly).
For cosmetic jaw slimming, concentrate the dose in the inferior masseter bulk (angle of mandible region). For dystonia, distribute more broadly across the muscle.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the lateral face overlying the mandibular ramus, with the probe parallel to the zygomatic arch
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the lateral face over the mandibular ramus, midway between the zygomatic arch and the angle of the mandible.

The masseter appears as a thick, rectangular hypoechoic muscle with internal hyperechoic septa (reflecting its multipennate architecture) directly superficial to the hyperechoic mandibular ramus cortex.
The mandibular ramus cortex is the deep landmark — a bright, continuous hyperechoic line with posterior acoustic shadowing. The needle target is the muscle belly superficial to this cortex.

Superficial to the masseter, identify subcutaneous fat and, posteriorly, the parotid gland as a more homogeneous, moderately hyperechoic structure with a different echotexture than the striated muscle.
Sliding the probe anteriorly, Stensen's duct may be visible as a thin hypoechoic tubular structure exiting the anterior parotid — avoid this region.
Ask the patient to clench and relax — the masseter will visibly thicken and thin, confirming identification.
safetyNotes: The parotid gland overlies the posterior masseter — deep injections in this area may inadvertently inject the parotid, causing sialadenitis or pain.
The transverse facial artery runs between the zygomatic arch and the parotid duct — use color Doppler to map it before injection.
The buccal branch of the facial nerve crosses the masseter superficially — staying deep within the muscle belly (not subcutaneous) reduces risk of facial nerve palsy.
Stensen's duct (parotid duct) crosses the mid-masseter anteriorly — keep injections in the posterior two-thirds of the muscle to avoid duct injury.

dosageNote: Per side. For bilateral bruxism, inject both sides. Reduce dose in smaller or elderly patients. Total bilateral session dose 50-100 U Botox.
probePlacementHint: Patient seated upright with jaw relaxed. Place probe transversely on lateral face over the mandibular ramus midway between zygomatic arch and mandibular angle. Photo from lateral view showing probe on cheek.
pearls: Ask the patient to clench — the masseter is the most powerful jaw closer and will bulge prominently, making landmark identification trivial
Stay in the lower two-thirds of the muscle and the posterior half to avoid Stensen's duct anteriorly and the zygomatic branch of CN VII superiorly
For oromandibular dystonia (jaw closing type), the masseter and medial pterygoid work synergistically — consider injecting both for refractory cases
Onset of effect for bruxism is typically 3-7 days; warn patients that jaw may feel weak when chewing tough food
supplies: Ultrasound machine with linear probe (optional but recommended)

name: Temporalis
pattern: Jaw clenching, bruxism, oromandibular dystonia (jaw closing type), temporal headache
landmarks: Temporal Fossa (palpate the flat concavity on the lateral skull above the zygomatic arch and anterior to the ear — the temporalis fills this entire fossa as a broad, fan-shaped muscle; ask the patient to clench their teeth and feel the muscle contract under your fingers)

Zygomatic Arch (palpate the bony ridge from the tragus anteriorly toward the cheekbone — the temporalis passes deep to the zygomatic arch to insert on the coronoid process of the mandible; the arch is the inferior boundary of the accessible muscle)
Superior Temporal Line (palpate the faint curved ridge on the lateral skull approximately 4-5 cm above the zygomatic arch — this marks the superior extent of the temporalis origin)
placement: Position the patient seated upright or supine with the jaw relaxed.
Ask the patient to clench their teeth — the temporalis will contract visibly in the temporal fossa, particularly the anterior fibers above and anterior to the ear.
Identify 2-3 injection sites distributed across the anterior, middle, and posterior portions of the muscle fan within the temporal fossa.
The anterior fibers (above the lateral orbital rim) are the most powerful jaw elevators and are the primary target for bruxism and jaw-closing dystonia.

Insert the needle perpendicular to the skull surface into the muscle belly at each site. Advance only 0.5-1 cm — the temporalis is thin (5-10 mm) overlying the temporal bone, and the needle will contact bone quickly.
CAUTION: The superficial temporal artery runs vertically in the subcutaneous tissue of the temple, anterior to the tragus. Palpate for its pulse and avoid it. Use color Doppler if using ultrasound.
Aspirate before each injection. Distribute the total dose across 2-3 sites.
setup: Ultrasound guidance can be helpful to confirm muscle thickness and identify the superficial temporal artery.
The temporalis is a broad, flat muscle — it is thin over most of the temporal fossa (5-10 mm thick), thickening inferiorly as it converges toward the coronoid process.
Palpate for the superficial temporal artery pulse anterior to the tragus — this vessel is the primary vascular risk in this injection.
In patients with temporal wasting (e.g., elderly, myopathic), the muscle may be very thin — reduce the injection depth and consider US to confirm adequate muscle bulk.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the temporal fossa, perpendicular to the fibers, above the zygomatic arch
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the temporal fossa above the zygomatic arch. Apply minimal pressure — the temporalis is thin and compresses easily.
The temporalis appears as a thin, flat hypoechoic muscle directly overlying the hyperechoic temporal bone cortex. Its fibers run in a fan-shaped pattern converging inferiorly.
The temporal bone cortex appears as a bright, continuous hyperechoic line with posterior acoustic shadowing — this is the deep safety boundary.
Superficial to the muscle, identify the subcutaneous fat and the temporalis fascia (a bright hyperechoic line).

Use color Doppler to identify the superficial temporal artery in the subcutaneous layer — it appears as a pulsatile vessel with red/blue flow, typically located anterior to the tragus and running superiorly.
Confirm by asking the patient to clench — the muscle will thicken slightly.
safetyNotes: The superficial temporal artery runs in the subcutaneous tissue — always palpate and/or use Doppler to map it before injecting. Intravascular injection may cause localized hematoma or, rarely, ischemic complications.
The deep temporal nerves (branches of V3) innervate the temporalis from its deep surface — staying within the superficial and mid-muscle belly avoids these nerves.
The middle meningeal artery runs inside the skull deep to the temporal bone — do not use excessive force or long needles that could penetrate the thin temporal bone, particularly in elderly patients.
The temporalis is thin — 5-10 mm in most patients. Use short needles and control depth carefully.

dosageNote: Per side. Distribute across 2-3 sites within the temporal fossa. Prioritize the anterior fibers for jaw-closing dystonia.
probePlacementHint: Patient seated upright with jaw relaxed. Place probe transversely in the temporal fossa above the zygomatic arch. Photo from lateral view showing probe on temple.
pearls: The temporalis and masseter are the two primary jaw closers — for refractory bruxism or jaw-closing dystonia, inject both muscles bilaterally
Always palpate for the superficial temporal artery pulse before injecting — it is the major vascular risk in this region
The anterior fibers are the strongest jaw elevators and the primary target; posterior fibers primarily retract the mandible
In migraine patients, temporalis injection sites overlap with PREEMPT protocol sites — 20 U per side (5 U x 4 sites) per the Allergan PREEMPT paradigm
supplies: Ultrasound machine with linear probe (optional but recommended)

name: Lateral Pterygoid
pattern: Jaw opening dystonia, jaw protrusion, oromandibular dystonia (jaw opening type), jaw deviation to contralateral side
landmarks: Coronoid Notch of the Mandible (palpate just anterior to the tragus of the ear and inferior to the zygomatic arch — ask the patient to open and close the jaw; the coronoid notch is the depression felt between the condylar process posteriorly and the coronoid process anteriorly when the jaw is partially open; this is the transcutaneous access point to the infratemporal fossa)
Zygomatic Arch (palpate the bony ridge running from the tragus anteriorly — the lateral pterygoid lies deep to the zygomatic arch and the mandibular ramus in the infratemporal fossa)

Mandibular Condyle (palpate the condyle just anterior to the tragus — ask the patient to open and close the mouth; the condyle translates anteriorly during opening; the superior head of the lateral pterygoid inserts onto the TMJ disc and condylar neck)
placement: Position the patient seated upright or supine with the jaw slightly open (to translate the condyle forward and open the coronoid notch).
EMG guidance is STRONGLY RECOMMENDED for this deep injection — the lateral pterygoid is not directly palpable and lies in the infratemporal fossa deep to the mandibular ramus.
TRANSCUTANEOUS APPROACH (preferred): Identify the coronoid notch by palpating the depression between the condyle and coronoid process just anterior to the tragus, below the zygomatic arch.

Insert a 25-27G, 1.5-2 inch needle through the coronoid notch, directing it medially and slightly anteriorly toward the infratemporal fossa. Advance approximately 2-3 cm until EMG activity confirms placement in the lateral pterygoid.

Ask the patient to open the jaw against resistance while monitoring EMG — the lateral pterygoid fires during jaw opening, jaw protrusion, and contralateral jaw deviation. This confirms correct placement.

INTRAORAL APPROACH (alternative): Open the jaw maximally to expose the pterygomandibular raphe region. Insert the needle lateral to the pterygomandibular fold, directing it posterolaterally toward the lateral pterygoid plate. This approach requires a cooperative patient and adequate oral opening.
Aspirate carefully before injecting — the maxillary artery and pterygoid venous plexus are in close proximity.
Inject the dose at a single site once EMG confirmation is achieved.
setup: EMG guidance is the STANDARD OF CARE for lateral pterygoid injection — this is a deep muscle that cannot be directly palpated or reliably targeted by landmarks alone.
A hollow EMG needle (Teflon-coated monopolar needle, 37 mm or longer) is preferred — it allows simultaneous EMG recording and toxin injection through a single needle.
The maxillary artery runs through or adjacent to the lateral pterygoid — aspiration is critical. The pterygoid venous plexus surrounds the muscle.

Differentiate from medial pterygoid: the lateral pterygoid fires on jaw OPENING and protrusion; the medial pterygoid fires on jaw CLOSING. Test jaw opening against resistance to confirm lateral pterygoid recruitment on EMG.
Warn the patient that temporary jaw weakness and difficulty with chewing may occur for 2-4 weeks after injection.
dosageNote: Per side. Lower doses are used due to the small muscle volume and proximity to the medial pterygoid. Always use EMG guidance.
probePlacementHint: EMG-guided injection — no ultrasound probe placement. Patient seated with jaw slightly open. Needle inserted through coronoid notch anterior to tragus, directed medially into infratemporal fossa.
pearls: The lateral pterygoid is the PRIMARY jaw opener and protruder — it is the key target for jaw-opening oromandibular dystonia and involuntary jaw protrusion
EMG confirmation is essential: the lateral pterygoid fires on jaw OPENING (distinguishing it from the medial pterygoid which fires on jaw CLOSING)
The maxillary artery and pterygoid venous plexus surround this muscle — aspiration is mandatory; be prepared for minor bleeding or hematoma
Unilateral lateral pterygoid injection can cause jaw deviation to the injected side — in unilateral jaw deviation dystonia, inject the contralateral lateral pterygoid
supplies: EMG machine with audio and visual display

name: Medial Pterygoid
pattern: Jaw clenching, oromandibular dystonia (jaw closing type), synergist with masseter for jaw elevation
landmarks: Angle of the Mandible — Medial Surface (palpate the mandibular angle from the submandibular region — the medial pterygoid inserts onto the medial surface of the mandibular angle and ramus, forming a muscular sling with the masseter on the lateral surface)

Pterygoid Fossa (the medial pterygoid originates from the medial surface of the lateral pterygoid plate and the pyramidal process of the palatine bone — these landmarks are deep and not directly palpable but define the superior attachment in the infratemporal fossa)

Submandibular Region (palpate the soft tissue medial and inferior to the angle of the mandible — the medial pterygoid bulk can sometimes be felt as a firm mass in this region when the patient clenches)
placement: Position the patient seated upright or supine with the jaw slightly open.
EMG guidance or ultrasound guidance is STRONGLY RECOMMENDED — the medial pterygoid is deep and not directly palpable from the skin surface.

SUBMANDIBULAR (EXTRAORAL) APPROACH (preferred for US guidance): Palpate the medial surface of the mandibular angle from the submandibular region. Place the ultrasound probe submandibularly to visualize the medial pterygoid on the deep (medial) surface of the mandibular ramus.

Insert a 25-27G, 1.5-2 inch needle from the submandibular region, directing it superiorly and laterally toward the medial surface of the mandibular ramus. Under ultrasound or EMG, advance into the medial pterygoid muscle belly.

TRANSCUTANEOUS LATERAL APPROACH (EMG guided): Insert through the coronoid notch (same entry as lateral pterygoid) but direct the needle more medially and inferiorly. EMG will show jaw-CLOSING activity (as opposed to jaw-opening activity from the lateral pterygoid). Ask the patient to clench to confirm.

INTRAORAL APPROACH (alternative): Insert the needle medial to the mandibular ramus, posterior to the last molar, directing laterally toward the medial surface of the ramus. This requires EMG confirmation.
Aspirate before injecting — the inferior alveolar artery and maxillary artery branches are nearby.
Inject at a single site once proper placement is confirmed.
setup: EMG guidance or ultrasound guidance is strongly recommended for medial pterygoid injection due to its deep location on the medial surface of the mandibular ramus.

The medial pterygoid is a jaw CLOSER — differentiate from the lateral pterygoid (jaw OPENER) by asking the patient to clench. The medial pterygoid fires vigorously during jaw clenching, not during jaw opening.
From the submandibular approach, the submandibular gland and facial artery are superficial structures to avoid — identify them with ultrasound before needle insertion.
The inferior alveolar nerve enters the mandibular foramen on the medial ramus surface — deep injections near the foramen risk temporary inferior alveolar nerve block causing lip and chin numbness.
The lingual nerve runs medial to the mandibular ramus — avoid directing the needle too far medially.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz) or curvilinear (5-8 MHz) for deeper imaging
ultrasound.orientation: Coronal oblique from the submandibular region, angled superiorly toward the medial surface of the mandibular ramus
viewSteps: Place the probe in the submandibular region below and medial to the angle of the mandible, angled superiorly.
Identify the mandibular ramus cortex as a hyperechoic line with posterior acoustic shadowing — this is the key deep landmark.
The medial pterygoid appears as a hypoechoic muscle mass on the medial surface of the mandibular ramus, deep to the submandibular gland.
The submandibular gland appears as a homogeneous, moderately echogenic structure superficial to the medial pterygoid in this view.
Use color Doppler to identify the facial artery, which loops through or under the submandibular gland, and branches of the inferior alveolar artery.
Ask the patient to clench — the medial pterygoid will contract, confirming identification.
safetyNotes: The facial artery loops through the submandibular region — always use color Doppler to map it before needle insertion.
The inferior alveolar nerve and artery enter the mandibular foramen on the medial ramus — injection near this foramen risks temporary nerve block and arterial puncture.
The lingual nerve runs medial to the mandibular ramus — avoid directing the needle too far medially.
The submandibular gland lies superficial to the muscle in the submandibular approach — pass through or around it to reach the medial pterygoid.

dosageNote: Per side. The medial pterygoid is smaller than the masseter — use lower doses. Consider co-injection with the masseter for refractory jaw-closing dystonia.
probePlacementHint: Patient seated with jaw slightly open. For US guidance: place probe in submandibular region below mandibular angle, angled superiorly. For EMG: needle through coronoid notch directed medially. Photo from submandibular view showing probe below jaw angle.
pearls: The medial pterygoid and masseter form a 'pterygo-masseteric sling' around the mandibular angle — together they are the primary jaw closers
For refractory jaw-closing dystonia, inject both the masseter AND medial pterygoid — the medial pterygoid is often the overlooked synergist
Differentiate from lateral pterygoid on EMG: medial pterygoid fires on jaw CLENCHING, lateral pterygoid fires on jaw OPENING
Temporary numbness of the lower lip (inferior alveolar nerve block) is a known risk — warn patients before injection
supplies: Ultrasound machine with linear probe (for submandibular approach)
EMG machine with audio/visual display (for transcutaneous approach)
Teflon-coated monopolar EMG needle (37 mm, 27G) or standard 27G needle (1.5-2 inch)

name: Orbicularis Oculi
pattern: Blepharospasm (involuntary forceful eye closure), hemifacial spasm
landmarks: Orbital Rim (palpate the bony rim surrounding the eye — the orbicularis oculi is a thin, flat sphincteric muscle that encircles the entire orbital aperture, with fibers originating from the medial orbital margin near the medial canthal tendon and the frontal bone)

Lateral Canthus (palpate the lateral angle where the upper and lower eyelids meet — the preseptal and pretarsal fibers converge here; the lateral orbital rim is a key injection landmark for the lateral canthal sites)

Medial Canthal Tendon (palpate a firm cord-like structure at the medial angle of the eye, just lateral to the bridge of the nose — this is the origin of the pretarsal orbicularis; avoid injecting directly on this tendon to prevent disruption of the lacrimal pump mechanism)
placement: Position the patient seated upright in a well-lit room. The patient should be relaxed with eyes gently closed or in their resting blepharospasm position.

The orbicularis oculi has three functional parts: PRETARSAL (over the tarsal plates, closest to the eyelid margin), PRESEPTAL (over the orbital septum), and ORBITAL (over the orbital rim and beyond). For blepharospasm, the PRESEPTAL and ORBITAL portions are the primary targets.

Standard blepharospasm injection sites (6 sites per eye, per Allergan PI): (1) Medial upper preseptal — 1 cm above the medial upper eyelid crease, (2) Central upper preseptal — 1 cm above the central upper eyelid crease, (3) Lateral upper preseptal/orbital junction — at the lateral orbital rim above the lateral canthus, (4) Lateral lower orbital — at the lateral orbital rim just below the lateral canthus, (5) Central lower preseptal — midway along the lower orbital rim, (6) Medial lower preseptal — near the medial lower orbital rim (USE CAUTION — lacrimal punctum is nearby).

Insert the needle subcutaneously (bevel up) at each site, directed AWAY from the globe. The muscle is extremely thin (1-2 mm) and lies just beneath the skin with no significant subcutaneous fat over the eyelids.
Inject 1.25-2.5 U Botox (or equivalent) per site. Use a tuberculin syringe for precise dosing at these low volumes.
At the medial lower site (site 6), inject at least 5 mm lateral to the lacrimal punctum to avoid weakening the lacrimal pump (pretarsal orbicularis), which can cause epiphora (tearing).

CRITICAL: Keep all injections superficial and OUTSIDE the orbital septum. Do NOT inject into the pretarsal portion of the upper lid — this risks ptosis from diffusion to the levator palpebrae superioris.
Apply gentle pressure with gauze at each injection site for 30 seconds to minimize bruising and reduce diffusion to adjacent muscles.
setup: This is a LANDMARK-GUIDED injection — ultrasound is NOT used for orbicularis oculi injections. The muscle is too thin and superficial for meaningful ultrasound guidance.
Use a 30G, 0.5-inch needle on a tuberculin (1 mL) syringe for precise volume control and minimal patient discomfort.

Reconstitute Botox at a concentration that allows injection of small volumes per site (e.g., 2.5 U per 0.1 mL = 100 U / 4 mL dilution, or 5 U per 0.1 mL = 100 U / 2 mL dilution). The Allergan PI recommends 1.25-2.5 U per site.
Avoid wearing contact lenses during the procedure — have the patient remove them before injection.
If treating hemifacial spasm (unilateral), inject only the affected side. If treating blepharospasm (bilateral), inject both eyes symmetrically.
dosageNote: Dosage is per SITE (6 sites per eye). Total dose per eye: 7.5-15 U Botox. Bilateral total: 15-30 U. Per the Allergan PI for blepharospasm, the initial recommended dose is 1.25-2.5 U per site. Do not exceed 5 U per site or 200 U total over 30 days.
probePlacementHint: Landmark-guided injection — no ultrasound used. Patient seated upright in well-lit room. Six injection sites distributed around the orbital rim: 3 upper (medial, central, lateral) and 3 lower (lateral, central, medial).
pearls: Inject into the PRESEPTAL and ORBITAL portions only — avoid the PRETARSAL upper lid to prevent ptosis from diffusion to the levator palpebrae superioris
Stay lateral at the medial lower site to avoid the lacrimal pump mechanism — weakening the pretarsal orbicularis medially causes epiphora (excessive tearing)
Apply digital pressure for 30 seconds after each injection to minimize ecchymosis and reduce diffusion — the periorbital skin is extremely thin and vascular
For hemifacial spasm, the orbicularis oculi is almost always the first and most prominently affected muscle — start here and add lower face muscles only if needed

name: Corrugator Supercilii
pattern: Blepharospasm (glabellar component), brow furrowing, glabellar dystonia
landmarks: Superomedial Orbital Rim (palpate the bony rim at the medial upper corner of the orbit — the corrugator supercilii originates from the medial end of the superciliary arch on the frontal bone, deep to the frontalis and orbicularis oculi)

Medial Eyebrow (palpate the medial third of the eyebrow — the corrugator runs laterally and superiorly from its origin to interdigitate with the frontalis and orbicularis oculi in the skin of the mid-eyebrow; ask the patient to frown forcefully to feel the muscle contract as a firm oblique band)

Glabella (palpate the smooth bony area between the eyebrows above the nasal root — the corrugator supercilii lies deep on both sides of the glabella, and its contraction produces vertical furrows between the brows)
placement: Position the patient seated upright. Ask them to frown forcefully — the corrugator creates the vertical (11 lines) furrows between the brows.
Identify the muscle by palpating a firm oblique band running from the medial orbital rim superolaterally toward the mid-eyebrow.
The standard injection site is at the medial eyebrow, approximately 1 cm above the superomedial orbital rim, at the junction of the medial and central thirds of the eyebrow.
Insert a 30G needle at a shallow angle (20-30 degrees from the skin surface) directed laterally along the corrugator muscle belly. Advance approximately 0.5 cm into the muscle.
Inject 5-10 U Botox per side (per the Allergan PI). A single site per side is typically sufficient, though some practitioners split across 2 sites for broader coverage.
CRITICAL: Stay above the orbital rim at all times — do NOT angle the needle inferiorly into the orbit. Diffusion to the levator palpebrae superioris can cause ptosis.
The corrugator lies deep to the frontalis — ensure the needle penetrates through the frontalis to reach the corrugator. Too-superficial injection will weaken only the frontalis.
Apply gentle digital pressure to the injection site for 30 seconds.
setup: This is a LANDMARK-GUIDED injection — ultrasound is NOT used. The corrugator is a small, superficial muscle easily identified by palpation during active frowning.
Use a 30G, 0.5-inch needle on a tuberculin (1 mL) syringe.
The corrugator lies DEEP to the frontalis and orbicularis oculi — it is the deepest muscle layer in the glabellar region. The injection must penetrate through the overlying muscles to reach it.
In blepharospasm treatment, the corrugator and procerus are often injected together with the orbicularis oculi as part of a comprehensive periorbital injection session (Allergan PI).
dosageNote: Per side. Per the Allergan PI for blepharospasm, inject 5 U at each corrugator site. For cosmetic use (glabellar lines), the standard FDA-approved dose is 4 U Botox per corrugator (bilateral total 20 U including procerus).
probePlacementHint: Landmark-guided injection — no ultrasound used. Patient seated upright, frowning to activate the corrugator. Injection at the medial eyebrow approximately 1 cm above the superomedial orbital rim.
