pearls: Ask the patient to frown hard — the corrugator produces the vertical '11 lines' between the brows and becomes palpable as an oblique cord at the medial eyebrow
The corrugator is DEEP to the frontalis — ensure the needle penetrates through the frontalis to reach it; superficial injection only weakens the frontalis and misses the target
In blepharospasm, the corrugator and procerus should be injected together with the orbicularis oculi for comprehensive glabellar control
Ptosis risk: keep the needle directed laterally and superiorly, NEVER inferiorly into the orbit — diffusion to the levator palpebrae is the most feared complication

name: Procerus
pattern: Blepharospasm (glabellar component), horizontal nasal root furrows, glabellar dystonia
landmarks: Nasal Bridge / Nasal Root (palpate the bony bridge of the nose between the eyes — the procerus is a small, thin, midline muscle that originates from the nasal bone and lateral nasal cartilage and inserts into the skin of the glabella between the eyebrows)

Glabella (palpate the smooth area between the eyebrows — the procerus inserts here, interdigitating with the frontalis and corrugator supercilii fibers; contraction produces horizontal wrinkles across the nasal root)
Nasion (palpate the depressed area at the junction of the nasal bones and the frontal bone — this approximates the center of the procerus muscle origin)
placement: Position the patient seated upright. Ask them to 'scrunch' their nose or pull their brows down toward the nose — the procerus creates horizontal wrinkles at the nasal root.
Identify the midline of the nasal root between the medial eyebrows. The procerus is a midline structure — a single midline injection is sufficient.
Insert a 30G needle perpendicular to the skin or at a shallow angle into the midline nasal root/glabella junction, advancing only 2-4 mm into the thin muscle belly.
Inject 5-10 U Botox as a single midline injection.
The procerus is extremely thin (1-3 mm) — deep insertion is not necessary and risks penetrating to the periosteum.
Apply gentle digital pressure for 30 seconds to minimize bruising and diffusion.
setup: This is a LANDMARK-GUIDED injection — ultrasound is NOT used. The procerus is a small, thin, midline muscle easily targeted by landmarks.
Use a 30G, 0.5-inch needle on a tuberculin (1 mL) syringe.
The procerus is the most superficial muscle in the glabellar region — it lies superficial to the corrugator supercilii.
In blepharospasm treatment, the procerus is typically injected as part of the glabellar complex along with the corrugator supercilii and orbicularis oculi.
Over-injection or diffusion can cause a 'flat' or 'frozen' appearance of the nasal root — start with lower doses in cosmetically conscious patients.
dosageNote: Single midline injection. Per the Allergan PI for blepharospasm, 5 U at the procerus is standard. For cosmetic use (glabellar lines), 4 U Botox at the procerus is standard (part of the 20 U glabellar complex with corrugators).
probePlacementHint: Landmark-guided injection — no ultrasound used. Patient seated upright, actively scrunching the nose to identify the procerus. Single midline injection at the nasal root/glabella junction.
pearls: The procerus is a MIDLINE muscle — a single midline injection is sufficient; bilateral injections are not needed
Ask the patient to scrunch the nose downward — horizontal wrinkles at the nasal root confirm procerus activation
The procerus is the most superficial glabellar muscle (superficial to the corrugators) — only 2-4 mm of needle depth is needed
In blepharospasm, always inject the procerus along with the corrugators and orbicularis oculi for comprehensive glabellar dystonia control

name: Frontalis
pattern: Brow elevation dystonia, forehead spasm, involuntary brow raising; NOTE: this is primarily a cosmetic target (horizontal forehead lines) and is NOT a typical spasticity target
landmarks: Frontal Belly of the Occipitofrontalis (palpate the forehead — the frontalis is a broad, flat muscle covering the entire forehead from the eyebrows to the anterior hairline; ask the patient to raise their eyebrows to feel it contract and see horizontal forehead lines form)
Eyebrow (palpate the eyebrow ridge — the frontalis inserts into the skin and subcutaneous tissue of the eyebrow, interdigitating with the orbicularis oculi and corrugator supercilii inferiorly)
Anterior Hairline (palpate the hairline on the forehead — the frontalis has no bony insertion superiorly; it transitions into the galea aponeurotica at approximately the anterior hairline)
placement: Position the patient seated upright. Ask them to raise their eyebrows forcefully — the frontalis will contract, creating horizontal forehead lines.

NOTE: The frontalis is NOT a typical spasticity target. It is injected for (1) brow dystonia (rare), (2) cosmetic forehead lines, or (3) as an adjunct in blepharospasm when frontalis overactivity compensates for orbicularis oculi weakness post-injection.
For brow dystonia or cosmetic treatment, identify 4-5 injection sites distributed evenly across the forehead, at least 2 cm above the eyebrow to minimize risk of brow ptosis.
Standard cosmetic approach: inject at 4-5 sites across the mid-forehead, each approximately 1.5-2 cm above the brow and spaced 1.5-2 cm apart.
Insert the needle intradermally or into the very superficial subcutaneous plane (the frontalis is only 2-4 mm thick). Direct the bevel up and advance 2-3 mm.
Inject 2-5 U Botox per site. The total dose is typically 10-20 U distributed across all sites.

CRITICAL: Do not inject within 2 cm of the brow — this causes brow ptosis (the frontalis is the ONLY brow elevator). Loss of frontalis function drops the brow and may worsen pre-existing dermatochalasis or upper lid heaviness.
In blepharospasm patients, injecting the frontalis after orbicularis oculi treatment can worsen brow ptosis — use extreme caution and low doses.
setup: This is a LANDMARK-GUIDED injection — ultrasound is NOT used. The frontalis is immediately subcutaneous across the entire forehead.
Use a 30G, 0.5-inch needle on a tuberculin (1 mL) syringe.

⚠️ IMPORTANT CLINICAL NOTE: The frontalis is the ONLY muscle that elevates the brow. Excessive weakening causes brow ptosis, which can be functionally and cosmetically significant. Always warn the patient about this risk.
In patients with heavy brows, low-set brows, or significant dermatochalasis, frontalis injection may cause the brow to drop into the visual axis — assess baseline brow position before injection.
The frontalis has no bony insertion — it inserts into the skin of the brow and the galea aponeurotica. Injections are necessarily into soft tissue only.
dosageNote: Total across all forehead sites (4-5 sites at 2-5 U each). For brow dystonia, use the minimum effective dose. For cosmetic treatment, 10-20 U total is standard. This is NOT a typical spasticity target.
probePlacementHint: Landmark-guided injection — no ultrasound used. Patient seated upright, raising eyebrows to activate frontalis. Four to five injection sites distributed across the mid-forehead at least 2 cm above the brow.
pearls: The frontalis is the ONLY brow elevator — over-injection causes brow ptosis, which is the #1 complication; always stay at least 2 cm above the brow
NOT a spasticity target — it is injected for cosmetic forehead lines, rare brow dystonia, or as part of migraine PREEMPT protocol (20 U total, 5 U per site x 4 sites)
In blepharospasm patients already receiving orbicularis oculi injections, adding frontalis can worsen brow ptosis — assess carefully before treating
Asymmetric injection or uneven dosing causes a 'Spock brow' (asymmetric lateral brow elevation) — distribute the dose symmetrically and include lateral sites

name: Platysma
pattern: Anterocollis (forward head flexion), neck banding, platysmal dystonia, involuntary neck tightening
landmarks: Mandibular Border (palpate the inferior border of the mandible — the platysma originates from the subcutaneous tissue and fascia overlying the upper pectoralis major and deltoid, and inserts onto the lower mandible, the skin and subcutaneous tissue of the lower face, and the modiolus at the oral commissure)

Anterior Neck Bands (ask the patient to grimace or forcefully depress the lower lip — the platysma contracts and its medial borders become visible as vertical bands or cords running from the clavicle to the chin in the anterior neck)

Clavicle (palpate the medial third of the clavicle — the platysma is a broad, thin, sheet-like muscle that originates from the fascia overlying the upper chest and drapes over the anterior neck; its inferior border is at or just below the clavicular line)
placement: Position the patient seated upright or supine. Ask them to grimace or depress the mandible with lips retracted to make the platysmal bands prominent.
For ANTEROCOLLIS: Identify the prominent platysmal bands in the anterior neck. These are typically most visible as vertical cords running from the clavicle to the chin.

Pinch the platysmal band between thumb and index finger to isolate it from the underlying strap muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid). The platysma is extremely thin (1-3 mm) and lies directly in the subcutaneous tissue.
Insert a 30G needle subcutaneously into each platysmal band, directing it along the length of the band. Advance only 2-3 mm — the muscle is immediately beneath the skin.
For anterocollis, inject 2-3 sites per band, with 2-4 prominent bands typically identified, for a total of 6-10 injection sites.
For NECK BANDING (cosmetic): Inject along the visible platysmal bands with smaller doses per site (2-5 U Botox per site).
CAUTION: The platysma overlies the external jugular vein, the anterior jugular veins, and the strap muscles. Do NOT inject deeply — stay strictly subcutaneous.
Aspirate before injecting — the external jugular vein is superficial and crosses the lateral neck under the platysma.
After injection, apply gentle pressure and have the patient remain upright for 5 minutes to minimize diffusion to the deeper neck musculature.
setup: This is predominantly a LANDMARK-GUIDED injection — the platysmal bands are visible and palpable in most patients.
Ultrasound can be used as an adjunct to differentiate the platysma from the underlying strap muscles if the bands are not clearly visible.
The platysma is extremely thin (1-3 mm) — use a short, fine-gauge needle and stay subcutaneous.

WARNING: Excessive dosing or diffusion to the deeper neck flexors (longus colli, longus capitis) or strap muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid) can cause dysphagia, neck flexion weakness, and aspiration risk. Use the minimum effective dose.

In anterocollis, the platysma is rarely the sole cause — it is typically injected as an adjunct to the deep neck flexors (longus colli/capitis). Consider whether the platysma or the deep flexors are the primary driver before treating.
For bilateral injections, use reduced doses per side to minimize the risk of dysphagia.
dosageNote: Total dose across all sites per side. For anterocollis, 25-50 U Botox per side distributed across 2-4 platysmal bands. For cosmetic neck banding, 2-5 U per site along each band (lower total dose). Use the minimum effective dose to avoid dysphagia.
probePlacementHint: Landmark-guided injection — platysmal bands are identified visually by asking the patient to grimace. Patient seated upright, grimacing to display anterior neck bands. Inject subcutaneously into each visible band.
pearls: Ask the patient to grimace or forcefully depress the lower lip — the platysmal bands will become visible as vertical cords in the anterior neck
The platysma is paper-thin (1-3 mm) and lies in the subcutaneous tissue — use short needles, stay superficial, and NEVER inject deeply to avoid the underlying strap muscles and swallowing musculature
For anterocollis, the platysma is usually an ADJUNCT target — the deep neck flexors (longus colli/capitis) are typically the primary drivers of forward head flexion
Dysphagia is the most feared complication — minimize dose, avoid bilateral high-dose treatment, and warn patients about potential swallowing difficulty for 2-4 weeks

name: Mentalis
pattern: Chin dimpling dystonia, involuntary chin puckering, mental crease deepening, lower lip protrusion
landmarks: Mental Protuberance (palpate the bony prominence at the center of the chin — the mentalis originates from the incisive fossa on the anterior mandible, just below the lower incisor roots, and inserts into the skin of the chin)

Chin Pad (palpate the soft tissue pad of the chin below the lower lip — the mentalis is a small, conical, paired midline muscle that lies within this chin pad; when it contracts, it dimples, puckers, or elevates the chin skin)

Labiomental Crease (identify the horizontal crease between the lower lip and the chin prominence — the mentalis lies deep to this crease; ask the patient to protrude the lower lip or 'pout' to see the chin dimple and wrinkle, confirming mentalis contraction)
placement: Position the patient seated upright. Ask them to 'pout' or push the lower lip forward — the mentalis will contract, producing the characteristic 'chin dimpling' or 'orange peel' appearance on the chin pad.
Identify the chin pad below the labiomental crease. The mentalis lies in the midline, one muscle on each side of the midline, converging at the chin.
For unilateral dystonia or dimpling, inject the affected side only. For bilateral chin dimpling, inject both sides.
Insert a 30G needle at the center of the chin pad, approximately 1 cm below the labiomental crease, directed toward the mandible. Advance 3-5 mm into the muscle belly.
Inject 2.5-5 U Botox per side as a single midline injection per side (2 injection sites total for bilateral treatment).

CAUTION: The mental nerve exits the mental foramen approximately at the level of the second premolar, below and lateral to the mentalis — avoid lateral injections near the premolar region to prevent mental nerve block (lower lip and chin numbness).
The depressor labii inferioris lies lateral and deep to the mentalis — excessive lateral or deep injection can weaken lower lip depression and impair oral competence.
Apply gentle pressure to the injection site for 30 seconds.
setup: This is a LANDMARK-GUIDED injection — ultrasound is NOT used. The mentalis is a small, superficial chin muscle easily identified by its characteristic 'dimpling' contraction.
Use a 30G, 0.5-inch needle on a tuberculin (1 mL) syringe.
The mentalis is small — use low doses to avoid diffusion to the orbicularis oris (causing oral incompetence) or the depressor labii inferioris (causing asymmetric lower lip).
In patients with mentalis dystonia, the chin dimpling and puckering is typically involuntary and socially distressing — even small doses of botulinum toxin can provide significant relief.
dosageNote: Per side. Bilateral total: 5-10 U Botox. Start at the lower end of the dosing range to minimize risk of oral incompetence. The mentalis is a small muscle — small doses are effective.
probePlacementHint: Landmark-guided injection — no ultrasound used. Patient seated upright, pouting to activate the mentalis and display chin dimpling. Inject at the center of the chin pad approximately 1 cm below the labiomental crease.
pearls: Ask the patient to 'pout' or push the lower lip out — the mentalis produces the classic 'orange peel' or dimpled chin appearance when it contracts
The mental nerve exits the mental foramen below the second premolar — stay in the midline and avoid lateral injections to prevent chin and lip numbness
Very low doses are effective for this small muscle — start with 2.5 U Botox per side and titrate up only if needed
Diffusion to the adjacent orbicularis oris can impair lip seal (oral incompetence, drooling) — stay within the chin pad and avoid directing the needle superiorly toward the lip

name: Adductor Brevis
landmarks: Pubic Tubercle (palpate the bony prominence on the superior pubic ramus medially — the adductor brevis originates from the body and inferior ramus of the pubis, just lateral to gracilis)
Adductor Longus Tendon (palpate the prominent cord in the proximal medial thigh with the hip slightly abducted — the adductor brevis lies immediately deep to adductor longus)
placement: Position the patient supine with the hip abducted 30-45 degrees, knee flexed, and the leg externally rotated (frog-leg position).
Identify the adductor longus tendon at its pubic origin — palpate the firm cord at the medial groin crease.
The adductor brevis lies DEEP to the adductor longus. It cannot be reliably distinguished by palpation alone.
USG is strongly recommended — insert the needle through the adductor longus to reach the adductor brevis layer deep to it.
Under ultrasound, advance the needle through the adductor longus until the tip crosses the fascial plane into the deeper adductor brevis muscle belly.
Confirm depth — adductor brevis lies between adductor longus (superficial) and adductor magnus/obturator externus (deep).
Aspirate before injecting. A single injection into the mid-belly is usually sufficient.
Avoid advancing too deeply past adductor brevis into the obturator externus or adductor magnus unless those are also targets.
setup: USG guidance is strongly recommended — adductor brevis is deep to adductor longus and cannot be reliably injected by landmark alone.
The obturator nerve (anterior branch) runs on the superficial surface of adductor brevis between it and adductor longus — identify it and avoid direct injection into the nerve.
The posterior branch of the obturator nerve runs between adductor brevis and adductor magnus — it may be visible deep to the target on US.
The profunda femoris artery and its branches run in this plane — use color Doppler to identify vessels before needle insertion.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the proximal medial thigh, at the level of the adductor longus origin, perpendicular to the muscle fibers
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the proximal medial thigh with the hip abducted.
Identify the adductor longus as the most superficial adductor — a large hypoechoic muscle belly with a prominent proximal tendon originating from the pubic tubercle.
Deep to adductor longus, identify a thin fascial plane — the adductor brevis lies immediately deep to this fascia as a smaller hypoechoic muscle.
The anterior branch of the obturator nerve may be visible as a small hyperechoic structure in the fascial plane between adductor longus and brevis.
Deep to adductor brevis, the adductor magnus fills the deepest compartment.
Use color Doppler to identify the profunda femoris artery and its perforating branches, which run in the inter-muscular planes.
safetyNotes: The anterior branch of the obturator nerve runs between adductor longus and brevis — identify it and keep the needle tip within the muscle belly, not in the inter-fascial plane.
The profunda femoris artery and perforating branches course through the adductor compartment — use Doppler before each needle insertion.
Do not advance past adductor brevis into the deeper compartment unless targeting adductor magnus.

dosageNote: Often co-injected with adductor longus and magnus for refractory scissoring.
probePlacementHint: Patient supine, frog-leg position. Place probe transversely on proximal medial thigh at the adductor longus origin. Photo from medial view showing probe on inner thigh.
pearls: The 'hidden' adductor — lies deep to adductor longus and is often missed when only the superficial adductor longus is injected
If scissoring persists after treating adductor longus alone, add adductor brevis (and magnus) to the injection set
Anterior branch of obturator nerve sits right on top of this muscle — always identify it on US before injecting
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Gracilis
pattern: Hip adduction + knee flexion (crosses both joints)
landmarks: Pubic Symphysis (palpate the midline bony prominence — gracilis originates from the inferior pubic ramus near the symphysis, the most medial of the adductor origins)
Pes Anserinus (palpate the medial tibial flare below the knee — gracilis inserts here along with semitendinosus and sartorius; it is the middle tendon of the three)
placement: Position the patient supine with the hip abducted 30-45 degrees and knee slightly flexed.
Identify the gracilis as the most superficial and medial muscle of the adductor group — it forms a thin strap running the full length of the medial thigh.
Palpate the thin muscle belly on the medial thigh — ask the patient to adduct the hip against resistance while you feel for contraction medially.
The injection site is the proximal third of the medial thigh, where the muscle belly is thickest.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin on the medial thigh into the superficial muscle belly.
Advance only 1-1.5 cm — gracilis is thin and superficial.
Aspirate before injecting. A single injection into the proximal belly is usually sufficient.
Stay superficial — the adductor longus and brevis lie deep to gracilis.
setup: Gracilis is superficial and thin — landmark-guided injection is often adequate, but USG can confirm you are in gracilis and not the deeper adductor longus.
The muscle is a thin strap — use low volume to avoid diffusion into adjacent adductors.
The saphenous nerve and great saphenous vein run in the medial thigh — they are anterior to gracilis but should be identified if using US.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the proximal medial thigh
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely on the proximal medial thigh with the hip abducted.
Gracilis appears as a thin, flat hypoechoic muscle strap on the most medial and superficial aspect of the thigh, directly under the subcutaneous tissue.
Deep to gracilis, the adductor longus is visible as a larger, thicker hypoechoic muscle.
The sartorius is visible anterolaterally — do not confuse the two (sartorius is more lateral and anterior).
To confirm: ask the patient to adduct the hip — gracilis will contract and thicken on real-time US.
safetyNotes: The saphenous nerve and great saphenous vein run in the subsartorial canal anterior to gracilis — identify them if visible and avoid.
Stay within the thin gracilis belly — deep penetration enters adductor longus.
The obturator nerve branches are deep to gracilis in the adductor compartment.

dosageNote: Thin muscle — conservative dosing to avoid spread to adjacent adductors.
probePlacementHint: Patient supine, frog-leg position. Place probe transversely on proximal medial thigh. Photo from medial view showing probe on inner thigh.
pearls: The only adductor that crosses both hip and knee — contributes to both scissoring AND knee flexion
Standard target in adductor injection sets alongside longus and magnus — don't forget it
Very thin and superficial — easy to over-penetrate into deeper adductors; use short needle and minimal depth
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
